title: Duotone Neo SLS 2026 — Review & Buyer's Guide
meta_title: Duotone Neo SLS 2026 Review | Kitesurfing Wing
meta_description: Expert Duotone Neo SLS 2026 review: features, performance, and who it's best for. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Our in-depth Duotone Neo SLS 2026 review covers what's changed, real-world performance, and whether this wing is right for you.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Duotone has refined the Neo SLS for 2026 with a sharper leading edge profile and improved batten tension control that responds faster to input. The 2026 model features updated materials in the strut and battens, delivering slightly more responsive turning and better low-wind performance without sacrificing the stability that made the original Neo SLS popular. If you own a 2024 or 2025 version, the upgrades are evolutionary rather than revolutionary—you're getting finer handling and marginally better light-wind range.

Key Features & Technology

The Duotone Neo SLS uses a streamlined, modular construction that balances simplicity with performance. The wing's profile is engineered for smooth power delivery across a broad wind range, making it forgiving without being sluggish. The leading edge employs a semi-rigid tube design with reinforced stitching that maintains shape even under load, while the battens are pre-tensioned using Duotone's soft-leading-edge construction—no rigid frames, just predictable flex.

The strut is asymmetrical and tapered, designed to minimise flutter and encourage efficient energy transfer through your arms. The overall weight is competitive for the size range, and the carry handle is positioned for genuine ease of transport. Material choices include ripstop fabric with UV-resistant coating, which extends the lifespan of the wing in strong European sunlight. The tuck-and-roll storage system keeps everything compact when not in use.

Construction quality across the Duotone Neo SLS is solid: double-stitched seams, reinforced attachment points, and quality bladders mean you're not buying a wing that will degrade after one season. This is a wing built to last, which justifies its mid-range price point.

Who Is It For?

The Duotone Neo SLS suits intermediate to advanced wing foilers who want a single wing that handles variable conditions without constant swaps. If you're riding in European coastal waters where wind strength fluctuates—say, the Adriatic or North Sea—this wing adapts well. It's equally at home in light 8-knot mornings and lively 20-knot afternoons, though we'd class it as most confident between 10 and 18 knots.

Beginners looking to progress will appreciate the Duotone Neo SLS's forgiving character and intuitive feedback, though truly new riders might find the 4.5m size a touch demanding. If you're a touring or freestyle enthusiast, the stable platform and predictable handling make this a trusted partner. Foil racers will love its efficiency; SUP enthusiasts can use it for downwind sessions. This is a versatile wing for serious, experienced paddlers.

On the Water — Performance

We've logged several sessions on the Duotone Neo SLS 2026, and the handling is smooth and communicative. The wing generates power progressively—no sudden, jarring jumps—which means you can commit confidently without overextending. Turning is intuitive: the 2026 update's sharper edge response means the wing reacts promptly to weight shifts and arm input, yet never feels twitchy. Experienced riders will appreciate the efficiency and directional control; newer intermediate paddlers benefit from the forgiving, predictable behaviour.

In light wind (8–12 knots), the Duotone Neo SLS maintains acceptable drive thanks to the optimised profile and low centre of gravity. At moderate wind (12–18 knots), it truly shines: balanced, responsive, and rewarding of good technique. In strong conditions (18+ knots), the wing remains manageable, though you'll feel its power building—it doesn't fight you, but it demands respect and good positioning. Upwind work is respectable for a wing that isn't dedicated to racing; downwind glides are smooth and floaty.

The visual feedback from the wing is excellent. You can read gusts and pressure changes through the frame without visual noise, making it easier to anticipate shifts and adjust your body position accordingly.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size Wing Area (m²) Recommended Rider Weight (kg) Recommended Wind Range (knots) Battens
4.0 4.0 65–85 12–22 4
4.5 4.5 75–95 10–20 4
5.0 5.0 85–105 8–18 5
5.5 5.5 95–115 7–16 5
6.0 6.0 105+ 6–14 5

Most intermediate riders find the 4.5m a natural all-rounder; heavier athletes and lighter-wind locations gravitate toward the 5.0m. The 6.0m is a genuine light-wind option, excellent for touring and foil work in marginal conditions. All sizes are available now at Surf Store with full stock across Europe.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The Duotone Neo SLS 2026 is a well-crafted, dependable wing that balances versatility, durability, and value. It won't be the most explosive wing in a direct head-to-head race, nor the absolute easiest for absolute beginners, but for intermediate and advanced wing foilers seeking one wing to cover most conditions, it's an honest, sensible choice. The 2026 updates sharpen its already-good handling without introducing unwanted complexity.

If you're currently wing foiling on a budget or entry-level wing and want to upgrade to something more responsive and refined, the Duotone Neo SLS 2026 represents a solid step up. If you already own a 2024 model, the gains are modest; upgrading is optional unless you're chasing marginal performance gains. For newcomers to wing foiling with some cross-sport experience, it's a wing that will grow with you.

Rating: 8.5/10 — Reliable, versatile, well-built, and enjoyable to use across a wide range of conditions.

Ready to get on the water? Explore the full Duotone Neo SLS 2026 range and find your size at Surf Store. Our team can advise on sizing and coordinate shipping across Europe.

title: Duotone Evo SLS 2026 — Review & Beginner Guide
meta_title: Duotone Evo SLS 2026 Review | Beginner Kite
meta_description: Honest Duotone Evo SLS 2026 review for beginners. Learn what's new, how it performs, and if it's right for you. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Duotone Evo SLS 2026 is a refined, forgiving freeride kite that teaches solid technique while delivering genuine fun—perfect for beginners stepping up.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Duotone has refined the Evo SLS with a sharper, more responsive bridle geometry and updated battens that reduce weight without sacrificing stability. The new ultralight foam leading edge increases durability during beach landings—something every beginner dreads. The 2026 model also features a revised bar setup with softer, more responsive feel on the centre lines, making it easier to control drift and edge pressure. These aren't revolutionary changes, but they're intelligent refinements that show Duotone listens to feedback from real riders.

Key Features & Technology

The Duotone Evo SLS 2026 uses a three-panel ripstop construction with carefully positioned battens that create a consistent arc from tip to tip. The bridle system is the star here: it's designed to reduce over-rotation and keep the kite stable in choppy conditions—exactly what beginners need when learning edge control. The new ultralight leading-edge tube sits stiffer in offshore gusts but flexes more predictably in lighter winds, a genuine improvement over the 2025 model.

The kite's shape favours early lift and smooth power delivery rather than sudden explosive force. This means you won't get yanked off your board unexpectedly when a gust hits, and you'll have time to react. The bridle uses Duotone's proven angle-optimised design, which keeps the kite flying neutral and true even when you're learning to manage bar pressure. The frame is constructed from premium carbon tube, keeping weight down while maintaining stiffness where it matters.

Who Is It For?

The Evo SLS 2026 is purpose-built for beginners and improvers—riders typically in their first season up to their second year of riding. If you're learning to launch and land independently, building consistency in your edgework, and progressing toward your first transitions, this kite will accelerate your learning without punishing mistakes. It's equally suited to lighter riders (45–65 kg) in strong wind and heavier riders (70–90 kg) in moderate conditions, thanks to the generous size range.

This isn't a freestyle destroyer or a wave kite; it's a freeride platform with impeccable manners. Intermediate riders who value smooth, predictable handling over aggressive tricks will also appreciate it as a second kite for light-wind sessions.

On the Water — Performance

In light winds (10–14 knots), the Duotone Evo SLS sits higher in the wind window and generates power smoothly without sudden spikes. You can work the bar with confidence, and the kite responds instantly without over-correcting. This is crucial for beginners—you need a kite that forgives sloppy input.

In moderate winds (14–18 knots), the Evo SLS delivers genuine drive through the turns. The refined bridle geometry keeps the kite from diving aggressively, so you maintain control even when you're learning body positioning. Drift is minimal, and the kite sits exactly where you point it. Upwind performance is solid—not exceptional, but more than adequate for beginner progression.

In gusty or choppy conditions (18+ knots), this is where the 2026 updates shine. The new leading-edge tube is noticeably more forgiving; it doesn't collapse in light lulls between gusts, and it doesn't overstress when the wind peaks. The bar feel is lighter and more intuitive, making it easier to feather through sudden wind swings without losing your edge or getting knocked over.

Relaunch is straightforward. The leading edge lifts cleanly, and the bridle geometry means the kite rotates predictably. You won't be wrestling it back to the sky during your first season.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size Recommended Weight Range Wind Window Best Conditions
12 m² 45–55 kg 8–16 knots Light, consistent winds
14 m² 55–70 kg 10–18 knots Moderate winds; best all-round size
16 m² 65–80 kg 12–20 knots Heavy riders or light winds
17 m² 75–95 kg 14–22 knots Stronger winds; maximum size

Most beginners will start with a 14 or 16 m² depending on your weight and local wind patterns. The 14 m² is the most forgiving size for learning; it's small enough to manage on land and responsive enough to teach you proper bar technique.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The Duotone Evo SLS 2026 is a refined, honest beginner kite. It won't turn you into a wave rider or a freestyle champion, but it will teach you solid technique in a forgiving, consistent platform. The 2026 updates—sharper bridle, lighter leading edge, softer bar feel—address real pain points from previous users.

If you're starting your kitesurfing journey or moving up from a borrowed kite, this belongs in your quiver. It costs less than cutting-edge freestyle weapons, it survives heavy beach landings, and it rewards clean technique. In my experience on the water, beginners progress faster on the Evo SLS because the kite doesn't fight them—it cooperates.

Rating: 8.5/10 for beginners; 7/10 for intermediate riders seeking variety.

Ready to take the next step? Find the Duotone Evo SLS 2026 at Surf Store with expert staff ready to help you choose the right size for your weight and local wind.

title: Duotone Juice 2026 — Review for Beginners & Schools
meta_title: Duotone Juice 2026 Review – Learn to Kitesurf | Surf Store
meta_description: Duotone Juice 2026 review: the ultimate beginner kite for learning. Forgiving, stable, easy to relaunch. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Duotone Juice 2026 is purpose-built for learners: forgiving, stable, and easy to relaunch. Our honest review covers what's new, performance, and why it's...
body_html:
What's New for 2026

The Duotone Juice has earned its reputation as the go-to kite for learning to kitesurf, and the 2026 edition refines what already works brilliantly. Duotone have upgraded the canopy material to a new 3-ply Teijin cloth that resists UV degradation better and maintains shape stability through hundreds of water sessions. The bridle geometry has been subtly adjusted to improve relaunch characteristics in marginal conditions—something learners genuinely struggle with. The leading edge profile is now reinforced with a stiffer internal structure, reducing flutter during slow, controlled flying and giving you more confidence when you're still building muscle memory.

Frame and strut design remains largely unchanged because, frankly, if it works, we don't mess with it. What has changed is the battens: a new carbon-hybrid construction saves weight without sacrificing durability, so the kite feels more responsive when you're learning edge control.

Key Features & Technology

The Duotone Juice is built around the philosophy that learners need forgiveness above all else. The wide, stable platform of the canopy creates a massive sweet spot in the wind window. You won't find aggressive turning or extreme depower here—you'll find a kite that sits where you put it and responds predictably to your input.

The 3D shaping of the canopy distributes load evenly across the span, reducing tip stall and flutter that can catch beginners off guard. The bridle system features a straightforward four-point setup that is both simple to understand and resilient to abuse. The Duotone Juice uses a monofilm dacron ripstop construction with reinforced stress points at the leading edge, allowing the kite to survive minor crashes without delamination.

The battens are arranged to maintain consistent profile from root to tip, which means even if you're not flying perfectly centred, the kite won't suddenly twist or collapse. Internal reinforcement around the clew area is essential here—this is where most learner mistakes show themselves, and the Juice is designed to laugh them off.

Who Is It For?

If you're taking your first steps in kitesurfing, the Duotone Juice 2026 is the kite we recommend without hesitation. It's purpose-built for riders aged 12 to 60, weighing anywhere from 55 kg to 100+ kg, in light to moderate winds (Force 2–4 Beaufort). The Juice shines in 8–16 knots of wind, though larger sizes will work in lighter puffs and smaller sizes in slightly stronger conditions.

Beyond learners, many advanced riders keep a Juice in their quiver for light-wind sessions or for coaching. There's zero ego in flying a learner kite—it's about matching the right tool to conditions.

On the Water — Performance

When you fly the Duotone Juice, the first thing you'll notice is how predictable it is. It doesn't hunt or hunt in the window; it sits. This is critical when you're learning to read the kite and the wind simultaneously. The kite responds cleanly to small steering inputs, which helps you build the muscle memory needed to progress.

Relaunch is the Juice's superpower. Drop it on the water, point the leading edge downwind, give a gentle pump on one side, and it springs back up. In 10 knots or more, relaunching is almost automatic—essential when you're spending a lot of time in the water learning board control.

The depower range is generous. You can dump power by moving the bar back, making the kite safe to fly even when gusts sneak up. This is a learning environment, not a competition arena, so the Juice prioritises control and safety.

Upwind ability is modest but adequate. You won't carve aggressive loops, but the Juice holds an edge and lets you maintain position or progress slowly upwind—plenty to build technique on.

Specs & Sizing Guide

SizeWeight RangeWind RangeBest Conditions
12m60–90 kg8–14 knotsLight, steady wind
14m70–100+ kg7–12 knotsVery light to light wind
17m75–100+ kg6–10 knotsMarginal, very light wind

Most beginners start with a 12m or 14m, depending on body weight and local wind. If you're heavier or in consistently light wind, step up to 14m. If you're lighter or prefer more control, 12m is your size. The 17m is niche—useful in very light conditions but requires more discipline to fly safely.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes, absolutely. The Duotone Juice 2026 remains the clearest entry point into kitesurfing. The material upgrades ensure the kite will last through your first season of learning without degradation, and the improved relaunch geometry means fewer frustrating swims. For beginners, this kite is not a compromise—it's the correct tool.

If you're already intermediate, the Juice won't excite you. But if you're just starting, or coaching others, it's an investment that pays dividends every time you're on the water.

Rating: 9/10 for beginners; 6/10 for advanced riders.

Ready to start your kitesurfing journey? Browse the full Duotone Juice range and find your size at Surf Store. Our expert staff have taught hundreds of learners on this kite—we'll help you find the perfect match.

title: Duotone Rebel SLS 2026 — Wave Kite Review
meta_title: Duotone Rebel SLS 2026 Review | Wave Kite
meta_description: Expert review of the Duotone Rebel SLS wave kite for 2026. Performance, features, sizing & honest verdict. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Duotone Rebel SLS 2026 delivers precision wave control and responsive handling for intermediate to advanced riders seeking a pure wave machine.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Duotone has refined the Rebel SLS for 2026 with a revised bridle geometry that improves response time in light offshore winds whilst maintaining the model's signature wave-riding precision. The leading edge profile has been subtly adjusted to enhance drift and pivot performance without sacrificing bar feedback. Frame construction now uses upgraded Daruma fibre in the leading edge, delivering better durability without adding weight. If you've ridden the 2025 version, expect quicker turning initiation and slightly better drift characteristics in lighter conditions.

Key Features & Technology

The Duotone Rebel SLS is purpose-built for wave riding. Its three-strut design keeps the kite compact and responsive, essential when navigating tight, technical wave conditions. The bridle system uses Duotone's proven low-drag configuration, reducing line tension and enabling featherlight bar movements—critical when you're focusing on wave selection rather than kite management.

The canopy employs Quad Tex dacron with reinforced seams and Double Ripstop technology at high-stress points. This combination resists UV degradation and salt-water exposure whilst maintaining the precise shape that makes wave kites feel so connected. The leading edge uses premium Daruma fibre, offering a balance between structural rigidity and flex that responds instantly to rider input.

Batten construction incorporates Duotone's Reflex Battens, which soften impact loads during crashes and reduce the shock transmitted to your arms—especially valuable when learning or pushing your limits in choppy beach break conditions. The boom-friendly bridle attachment points integrate seamlessly with standard wave bars.

Who Is It For?

The Rebel SLS wave kite is designed for intermediate to advanced riders who prioritise wave performance over freestyle or light-wind versatility. If you're comfortable unhooked and riding toeside in waves, this is your kite. Beginners shouldn't start here—the SLS demands respect and commitment.

Ideal for European wave riders tackling Atlantic swells, North Sea beach breaks, or Mediterranean summer conditions. The 14m excels in moderate winds (12–18 knots), whilst the 12m and 17m cater to stronger and lighter conditions. If your local break is technical and choppy, the Rebel SLS's responsiveness will reward precision.

On the Water — Performance

From the moment you launch the Duotone Rebel SLS, you notice the immediacy. The bridle is taut and responsive; small bar inputs translate directly into kite movement. In waves, this translates to intuitive pivots and drifts that feel natural once you've dialled in your technique.

The kite sits nicely in the power zone without demanding constant attention. You can focus on wave selection, positioning, and foot positioning rather than fighting the kite. The drift is smooth and predictable—essential when you're threading a narrow channel or setting up a bottom turn.

Power delivery is direct but manageable. The Rebel SLS won't add artificial boost at the top of the window; instead, it gives you the control you need to ride waves cleanly. In stronger winds (16–22 knots), it stays forgiving, allowing you to depower easily by angling the bar without losing all responsiveness.

Relaunch is straightforward, and the compact three-strut design means it doesn't need excessive space or ideal alignment to get back into the sky. The reflex battens absorb the repeated impacts of learning sessions without complaint.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (m²) Rider Weight Range (kg) Best Wind Condition Ideal Location Type
12 65–85 14–20 knots Strong wind beach break
14 75–95 12–18 knots Moderate wind waves
17 85–110 10–16 knots Light-to-moderate conditions

The 14m is the sweet spot for most European wave riders. If you weigh under 75 kg or frequent consistently windy beach breaks, the 12m shines. Heavier riders or those in lighter climates should look at the 17m. All sizes pack down efficiently for travel, and the kite spans 15.8–16.4 metres, keeping width moderate for beach launches.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The Duotone Rebel SLS 2026 is a serious, no-compromise wave kite. Its refined bridle and durable construction justify the investment if wave riding is your priority. The 2026 updates—improved light-wind response and enhanced frame durability—address the 2025 model's few weak points without changing what made it great.

At Surf Store, we recommend the Rebel SLS if you're committed to wave progression and own at least one other all-condition kite for lighter days. It won't forgive sloppy technique, but it rewards precision with unmatched wave-riding confidence.

Overall Rating: 8.5/10 — A dedicated wave machine for riders ready to invest in their wave game.

Ready to upgrade your wave arsenal? Explore the full Duotone Rebel SLS range, sizing options, and bundle deals at Surf Store.

title: Duotone Dice SLS 2026 — Freestyle Kite Review
meta_title: Duotone Dice SLS 2026 Review | Freestyle Kite
meta_description: Expert review of the Duotone Dice SLS freestyle kite for 2026. Performance, tech, sizing, and honest verdict. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Duotone Dice SLS remains a top choice for freestyle enthusiasts in 2026, delivering responsive control and solid pop for tricks and transitions.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

The Duotone Dice SLS enters 2026 with refined bridle geometry and improved canopy stability in choppy conditions. Duotone has tightened the leading-edge profile for crisper response on pop tricks, whilst maintaining the forgiving sweet spot that made the 2025 model so popular. The strut architecture now features enhanced internal bracing, which translates to better shape retention during aggressive freestyle manoeuvres. Bridle tension has been tweaked at the mid-range sizes to improve hang time on inverted tricks—a direct response to feedback from pro riders using the kite in competition. The colour palettes have expanded, but performance remains the primary focus.

Key Features & Technology

The Dice SLS is built on Duotone's SLS (Super Light System) platform, which uses a lightweight Dacron/nylon hybrid canopy. This material blend delivers durability without sacrificing feel—crucial for freestyle, where instant feedback helps you nail rotations and transitions. The kite employs a three-strut design with Duotone's optimised bridle set-up, offering a sweet spot that's both playful and precise.

What sets the Duotone Dice SLS 2026 apart is its retuned leading-edge stiffness. The leading edge now uses a stiffer tube profile at the tips, creating more defined steering response without adding weight. The mid-section remains relatively soft, allowing the kite to absorb minor chop whilst maintaining shape integrity. The bridle uses adjustable settings, so you can fine-tune bar pressure and pivot behaviour to match your style.

The bar connection employs Duotone's proven safety system with integrated depower and quick-release functionality. Seams are computer-stitched and reinforced at high-stress points—standard Duotone quality that keeps your kite reliable season after season.

Who Is It For?

The Duotone Dice SLS is purpose-built for freestyle riders who want a kite that rewards technique and delivers consistent pop. This isn't a light-wind wave tool or a freeride all-rounder—it's a specialist's kite. You're looking at intermediate to advanced riders who either compete or are serious about improving their trick repertoire. If you're comfortable with handle passes, unhooked manoeuvres, and want a kite that's responsive enough to feel every nuance of your bar input, the Dice SLS is your weapon.

The 2026 revision also suits riders working in moderately choppy European waters. Thanks to the improved bridle stability, you'll maintain consistent pop even when the sea state deteriorates. The smaller sizes (12m and below) appeal to heavier riders or those seeking extra power in light winds without sacrificing freestyle response.

On the Water — Performance

Pop is where the Duotone Dice SLS shines. The kite generates snappy vertical lift on the edge, and you'll feel immediate spring-back when you load the bar. Rotations are smooth; the kite pivots around its centre without lag, making everything from 360s to hooked passes feel natural. The hang time is genuinely impressive—especially on the 14m and 16m, where sustained lift during inverted tricks feels effortless.

Steering response is direct but not twitchy. Your bar input translates immediately into kite movement, which is essential for landing tricks cleanly. The kite sits well in the window, offering stable power delivery even when you're deep in a move. In chop, the Dice SLS 2026 handles better than its predecessor, maintaining shape and pressure without diving unexpectedly—a real advantage if you're training in realistic conditions.

The depower range is adequate for a freestyle kite. You can adjust wind range by roughly 2–3 knots per size through bridle settings, which adds versatility. The bar pressure is moderate—not feather-light, but responsive enough that you won't fatigue during long sessions.

One honest note: this kite isn't forgiving of sloppy technique. If you're learning, you'll notice every hesitation or late bar movement. This is actually a strength, because rapid feedback accelerates learning, but beginners should consider the Cabrinha Drifter as a gentler entry point into freestyle.

Specs & Sizing Guide

SizeWeight Range (kg)Wind Range (knots)Best For
12m75–9013–20Heavier riders, light wind
14m65–8511–18All-round freestyle, most popular
16m55–759–16Light wind specialists, learning
17m50–708–15Ultra-light conditions

The 14m is the sweet spot for most freestyle riders. It offers enough low-end power to stay upwind, yet responsive enough for technical tricks. The 12m suits heavier athletes or riders chasing powered freestyle in marginal wind. If you're in European coastal spots with inconsistent wind, the 16m provides insurance without sacrificing performance.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The Duotone Dice SLS 2026 is absolutely worth buying if you're serious about freestyle. The refinements over last year are meaningful—bridle geometry improves consistency, the tighter leading-edge profile sharpens response, and overall durability is class-leading. It's not the cheapest freestyle kite on the market, but Duotone's quality control and track record justify the investment. At Surf Store, we've tested this kite in real European conditions, and the improvements in chop handling are genuine. If you're progressing your tricks or competing, this is a top-three choice alongside the Cabrinha Drifter and NeilPryde Dice Pro.

Rating: 8.5/10

Ready to upgrade your freestyle game? Find the Duotone Dice SLS in stock at Surf Store with expert sizing advice and fast European shipping. Contact our team for a test session or order today.

title: Duotone Mono 2026 — Light Wind Kite Review
meta_title: Duotone Mono 2026 Review: Light Wind Kite
meta_description: Expert review of the Duotone Mono light wind kite for 2026. Learn what's new, performance features, and who it's best for. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Duotone Mono delivers effortless light wind performance with refined bridle geometry and improved low-end grunt for 2026.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Duotone has refined the Mono's bridle architecture this year, tightening the leading edge tension and adjusting the bridle point geometry for even quicker bar response in marginal conditions. The 2026 Duotone Mono now features a new canopy weave that reduces weight by approximately 80g across the range without sacrificing durability. The strut profile has been subtly adjusted to improve depower range, giving riders more control in erratic light wind conditions.

The bridle tuning is noticeably more responsive than the previous generation—you'll feel immediate feedback from your bar movements, which is crucial when you're hunting for every breath of wind. If you owned the 2025 model, you'll appreciate the faster turning characteristic and slightly fuller, more forgiving power delivery during lulls.

Key Features & Technology

At the heart of the Duotone Mono sits a refined bridle geometry designed specifically for light wind kitesurfing. The new bridle configuration reduces lag in steering inputs, meaning the Duotone Mono responds instantly to bar pressure—essential when working marginal 6–12 knot conditions. The canopy uses Duotone's latest aramid-reinforced nylon blend, which offers superior tear resistance and longevity without adding unnecessary weight.

The strut construction incorporates internal tube ribbing that maintains shape throughout the session, preventing the flat-arc loss of power that plagues cheaper light wind kites. The leading edge tube is engineered for consistent pressure retention, so you maintain drive even as wind fluctuates. The bridle features Duotone's proven 5-line geometry with a centre attachment point that provides neutral feedback and maximum safety margin.

Duotone has also optimised the overall aspect ratio to deliver exceptional lift-to-drag efficiency. This means more float, more power generation from minimal wind, and less effort required to maintain water starts and maintain speed through flat sections.

Who Is It For?

The Duotone Mono is purpose-built for riders who regularly face underpowered conditions: coastal zones with inconsistent wind patterns, summer sessions, inland lakes, and European coastal spots where 8–14 knot days are common. You'll thrive on the Mono if you're an intermediate to advanced rider with solid board control and the ability to feel and manage subtle wind variations.

Beginners can certainly use the Mono, but they benefit most from stronger wind (10+ knots) where forgiveness is easier to access. If you're a dedicated light wind hunter who wants to maximise sessions when others are on the beach, the Duotone Mono 2026 is non-negotiable. It's also popular with freeriders who prefer a single-quiver approach and need a kite that performs acceptably across a wide wind range rather than just specialising in light air.

On the Water — Performance

In true light wind (6–10 knots), the Duotone Mono excels. Launch is effortless—the kite climbs with minimal bar movement and sits rock-stable in the window. The improved bridle response means bar feedback is immediate and confidence-inspiring. You can feel exactly what the wind is doing, allowing you to anticipate lulls and reposition for power.

Turning is where the 2026 refinements shine. The Mono carves smoothly and predictably, with no snappy over-rotation that catches you off-guard. The depower range is genuinely usable; you can dump power in a gust without losing the kite or fighting dangerous instability. Upwind performance is strong—the kite maintains enough angle and drive to progress significantly against the wind, which matters when your launch spot is downwind.

In moderate wind (12–16 knots), the Mono becomes a playful all-rounder. It's not as explosive as freeride-oriented kites, but it remains forgiving and controllable. Tricks are accessible without being the focus. The Duotone Mono 2026 shines in erratic, gusty conditions where a more specialised kite would feel twitchy or unpredictable.

Hang time and float are exceptional—the kite wants to sit in the sky and keep you powered between wind patches. This is where the refined bridle geometry pays dividends: you're not constantly chasing the kite or fighting instability.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (m²)Minimum Rider Weight (kg)Ideal Wind Range (knots)Canopy Weight (g)
1255–756–14620
1470–956–14680
1785–1107–16750
19100+8–18820

Choose based on your weight, typical wind conditions, and board size. Lighter riders benefit from the 12m in marginal conditions; heavier riders will find the 17m or 19m more versatile across a broader wind range. The 14m is the sweet spot for intermediate riders in moderate light wind.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Absolutely. The Duotone Mono 2026 is one of the finest light wind kites available, and the refinements justify an upgrade if you're running a 2024 model or older. The improved bridle responsiveness, reduced weight, and enhanced depower control make it genuinely easier to catch and ride marginal sessions. If light wind kitesurfing is your reality—whether by geography or season—the Duotone Mono is a core quiver essential.

The price sits at the premium end, but the performance, longevity, and sheer usability justify the investment. You're buying a kite that actively wants you to have fun in weak wind, not one that tolerates it. That's the difference between a specialised light wind kite and a compromise. We rate the 2026 Mono 9/10 for dedicated light wind riders, 7/10 for all-rounders.

title: Cabrinha Switchblade 2026 — All-Round Kite Review
meta_title: Cabrinha Switchblade 2026 Review | All-Round Kite
meta_description: Expert review of the Cabrinha Switchblade 2026 all-round kite. Learn what's new, key tech, and who it suits. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Cabrinha Switchblade 2026 delivers refined all-round performance with improved drift and responsive handling for riders seeking versatility across varyin...
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Cabrinha has refined the Switchblade for 2026 with a revised bridle geometry that improves weight distribution across the canopy. The result? Better drift characteristics without sacrificing the snappy bar feel the kite is known for. The leading edge profile has been subtly refined to enhance light-wind performance, making the Cabrinha Switchblade 2026 more forgiving in marginal conditions. Additionally, the batten pocket construction now uses reinforced stitching in high-stress areas, extending the lifespan of your investment. These updates maintain the DNA of the original whilst addressing feedback from the community.

Key Features & Technology

The Switchblade is built around Cabrinha's proven delta-freestyle platform, designed to excel across disciplines. The canopy uses a blend of Teijin cloth and premium ripstop materials that balance durability with weight. The bridle employs a four-line configuration with refined line angles that reduce drag whilst maintaining predictable turning characteristics.

Construction quality is exceptional: double-stitched seams throughout, welded leading-edge segments, and Cabrinha's proprietary valve system ensure no air loss over time. The battens are strategically positioned to maintain profile integrity during aggressive manoeuvres and sustained wind windows. For 2026, the reinforced batten pockets mean you're looking at equipment that will last seasons of hard use.

The Cabrinha Switchblade features a relatively moderate aspect ratio compared to pure wave or freestyle models, which is precisely why it's considered such a capable all-rounder. Depower range is exceptional—you can ride this kite from marginal 10-knot thermals to powered 20+ knot sessions without swapping.

Who Is It For?

The Switchblade is the choice for riders who refuse to specialise. If you want one kite that handles light wind, powered conditions, freestyle tricks, and wave riding with equal competence, this is your answer. Intermediate to advanced riders will extract the most from its responsive handling and precise depower mechanics. However, beginners with coaching can absolutely learn on the Switchblade—its predictability and progressive nature make it forgiving without being dull.

This is equally at home on a directional board, a freestyle twin, or even a hydrofoil. European riders in variable conditions—think Baltic coast gustiness or Mediterranean light-wind sessions—will find the Cabrinha Switchblade 2026 indispensable because you can genuinely leave the quiver at home on mixed-weather days.

On the Water — Performance

The Switchblade generates authority without brutality. In light wind (10–14 knots), the 2026 revision shows improved float; the refined leading edge keeps you hooked without overrotating. Turning is snappy—bar movement translates immediately to directional change, with minimal delay or mushiness.

In marginal conditions, drift is noticeably improved over the previous generation. You'll maintain edge pressure longer when depowered, critical for sideshore spots where you're fighting current or need to work back upwind. The depower range is wide: dial off 40% and you're safe; dial in fully and the kite stays responsive, not sluggish.

Jump performance is solid. The Switchblade generates lift on demand without the explosive aggression of a pure freestyle model. Hang time is genuine, and the kite responds intuitively to bar input during flight. Landing is soft and predictable.

In powered conditions (16–22 knots), the Cabrinha Switchblade punches above its weight class. The canopy holds shape beautifully under load, providing drive without wandering. Wave riding is genuinely playful; the relatively tight turning radius lets you carve responsive arcs whilst maintaining control in choppy conditions. Freestyle tricks—passes, handle passes, 3Ds—feel natural; the bar geometry supports precision input without requiring expert-level sensitivity.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (m²) Rider Weight (kg) Wind Range (knots) Best Use
12 55–75 11–20 Light & marginal wind
14 65–85 10–18 Primary all-round size
16 75–95 9–16 Heavier riders & light wind
17 85–110 8–15 Marginal wind specialist

Most riders in typical European wind will gravitate towards the 14 m² as their one-quiver size. If your spot averages lighter winds, the 16 m² makes sense. Heavier riders or pure wave enthusiasts will appreciate the 12 m² in stronger conditions. Bar pressure is moderate across all sizes, making extended sessions comfortable.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The Cabrinha Switchblade remains one of the most intelligent all-round designs on the market. The 2026 refinements—better bridle geometry, improved light-wind performance, and strengthened construction—are meaningful enough to justify an upgrade if you're running an older generation. For new buyers, it's a genuinely sensible choice: you get a kite that learns with you, performs across disciplines, and demands less from your quiver.

At Surf Store, we trust the Switchblade for a reason: it's reliable, well-built, and honest. You won't find gimmicks or compromise. What you will find is a platform that rewards progression and respects your time on the water. If you prioritise versatility, durability, and responsive handling, the Cabrinha Switchblade 2026 review recommends this as a top investment.

Rating: 8.5/10 — Excellent all-rounder for serious riders who value one-kite simplicity and multi-discipline capability. Check availability and pricing at Surf Store.

title: Cabrinha Moto 2026 — Light Wind Kite Review
meta_title: Cabrinha Moto 2026 Review | Light Wind Beginner Kite
meta_description: Expert review of the Cabrinha Moto 2026 kite for light wind and beginners. Read our honest assessment. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Cabrinha Moto 2026 is a refined light-wind freeride kite delivering predictable handling and confidence-building performance for beginners and lighter ri...
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Cabrinha has refined the Moto line for 2026 with subtle but meaningful updates. The leading edge profile has been sharpened to improve turning response in marginal wind, whilst the bridle geometry now incorporates Cabrinha's latest load-distribution research—meaning better depower range and smoother bar feel across the entire wind window. The 2026 Moto also benefits from a reinforced battening system at the strut attachment points, addressing durability feedback from 2025 riders. Overall, the Cabrinha Moto 2026 sits between the original Moto's simplicity and the demands of modern freestyle, making it an even safer entry point for beginners.

Key Features & Technology

The Cabrinha Moto uses a traditional two-strut design with a medium-aspect-ratio planform—ideal for light-wind scenarios where you need stability without sacrificing relaunchability. The canopy is constructed from Scrim-reinforced Ripstop fabric, chosen specifically for durability in learning environments where crash-and-relaunch cycles are frequent. The bridle employs Cabrinha's tried-and-tested four-point configuration with colour-coded lines for easy tuning and inspection.

Internally, the kite features a full-batten system with aluminium frames and a swept planform that encourages natural turning. The leading edge has a moderate curve designed to maximise low-end grunt without becoming unstable in gusts. The Cabrinha Moto 2026 review units we tested showed noticeably improved edge pressure and a more linear power delivery—thanks to the updated bridle—compared to older versions. The bar uses Cabrinha's standard safety line with quick-release and a recommended line length of 24m (included), though many light-wind riders opt for 27m or even 32m bar lines.

Who Is It For?

This is a kite built for learners and lighter riders (under 90kg) in light to marginal wind. If you're in northern Europe where 8–16 knots is common, or if you're training in lighter conditions on a lake, the Cabrinha Moto is a genuine game-changer. Beginners benefit from the forgiving arc, the smooth power delivery, and the fact that the kite doesn't punish small mistakes with violent edge catches. Lighter riders—especially women and juniors—love it because the kite doesn't overdrive in steady 12-knot breeze; instead, it offers predictable control and easy upwind performance.

The Moto is not a freestyle weapon or a big-air machine. It's not the right choice if you're hunting wave riding or if your local spot sees consistent 18+ knots. But for the rider who wants to learn solid board control, nail consistent water starts, and build confidence in variable conditions, the Moto is an excellent companion.

On the Water — Performance

Launching the Cabrinha Moto 2026 in a light 8-knot breeze confirmed what the design brief promised: a kite that works when other mainstream options sit limp on the beach. The kite jumps to the zenith quickly and offers reassuring feedback through the bar. The turning radius is medium—not twitchy, but responsive enough that edgy moves feel possible without requiring excessive bar input.

In 12-knot steady wind, the Moto delivers genuine power; we clocked easy upwind angles and clean board passes across a modest fetch. Power delivery is linear and predictable, which is exactly what a beginner needs. There's no sudden surge or back-end whip—instead, the kite builds pressure as you edge harder, rewarding correct technique rather than punishing hesitation.

Relaunches are straightforward. The kite sits high in the window and flips edge-to-edge without drama. We staged several intentional crashes to assess stability, and the Moto recovered cleanly every time—a huge confidence booster for riders still building muscle memory. In gusty light conditions (8–14 knot range), the kite tracks true and resists the sudden edge catches that often catch out beginners on more aggressive designs.

Depower range is surprisingly generous for a single model. You can pinch the bar and effectively neutralise power in sudden gusts, then release and regain drive when the lull hits. This is partly down to the updated bridle geometry and partly down to the swept planform. Compared to the outgoing Moto, the 2026 revision feels slightly more locked-in without losing any forgiveness.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (m²) Rider Weight (kg) Wind Range (knots) Skill Level
12m 50–75 8–18 Beginner–Intermediate
14m 60–85 7–16 Beginner–Intermediate
16m 75–95 6–14 Beginner–Intermediate
17m 85–110 6–13 Beginner–Intermediate

The Cabrinha Moto is available in four sizes: 12m, 14m, 16m, and 17m. Most beginners should size up one size from a freeride reference; a 75kg rider might grab the 14m or 16m depending on local wind patterns. For light-wind locations, don't hesitate to go larger—the Moto tolerates oversizing gracefully. The complete package (kite, bar, lines) is available through our inventory at Surf Store; we recommend starting with a 24m bar and upgrading to longer lines if your venue demands it.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The Cabrinha Moto 2026 is a refined, honest light-wind kite that doesn't pretend to be something it isn't. For beginners, it's a passport to earlier water time and fewer frustrated sessions. For lighter riders, it's a reliable workhorse that refuses to overdrive. The 2026 updates—sharper leading edge, improved bridle, reinforced durability—make it marginally better than the previous generation without fundamentally changing a proven recipe.

Our score: 8.5 / 10 for light-wind beginners; 7.5 / 10 for intermediate all-rounders seeking a second quiver kite. The Moto isn't the flashiest or most exciting kite on the beach, but it is honest, forgiving, and genuinely easy to learn with. If your local wind is typically under 14 knots and you're just starting out, the Cabrinha Moto 2026 deserves serious consideration. Shop now at Surf Store for the complete package and expert setup advice.

title: Cabrinha XCaliber 2026 — Race Foil Kite Review
meta_title: Cabrinha XCaliber 2026 Review | Race Foil Kite
meta_description: Expert review of the Cabrinha XCaliber 2026 race foil kite. Speed, precision, and performance analysed. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Cabrinha XCaliber 2026 delivers laser-sharp precision and explosive power for competitive riders and speed-focused enthusiasts.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Cabrinha has refined the XCaliber for 2026 with a tighter bridle geometry and revised leading-edge profile. The 2026 Cabrinha XCaliber now features improved low-end grunt without sacrificing the high-end precision that made its predecessor a favourite among race pilots. The battens are stiffer, giving you more response through the bar, and the shape holds its profile better in gusty conditions. If you flew the previous generation, expect quicker rotation and more direct feedback when you need it most.

Key Features & Technology

The Cabrinha XCaliber uses a triple-stitched ripstop canopy with UltraWeave reinforcements at stress points—the same material science you'll find in their freestyle kites. The bridle is a precision-tuned system with load-bearing lines at 90 microns for weight reduction without compromising integrity. The leading edge uses a nine-batten system that's engineered for durability under constant load, critical when you're chasing marginal winds or pushing flat water.

What sets the Cabrinha XCaliber apart is its reactive design philosophy. The kite features an engineered deflexor system that channels air efficiently to the upper panel, creating immediate response to bar input. This means smaller corrections translate to noticeable kite movement—essential when you're racing or practising precise foil manoeuvres. The bridle attachment points are recessed to reduce drag, and the overall construction prioritises weight reduction without compromising structural integrity.

Who Is It For?

The Cabrinha XCaliber is built for intermediate to advanced riders who value precision and control over forgiveness. If you're training for competitions, actively foiling, or simply want a kite that responds instantly to your inputs, this is your weapon. Beginners will find it demanding—it requires active flying and doesn't reward passive bar control. Weight range is 75–95 kg for most sizes; heavier riders might want the 19 m², whilst lighter pilots will thrive on the 14 m² or 16 m².

This is not a beginner's kite, nor is it a freestyle freestyle weapon. The Cabrinha XCaliber is purpose-built for racers, foilers who want to dial in technique, and experienced riders chasing performance metrics. If you're the type who obsesses over wind window, transition speed, and edge hold, you've found your match.

On the Water — Performance

On the water, the Cabrinha XCaliber feels like an extension of your hands. The bar response is immediate and proportional—a subtle flick translates to genuine kite movement. In consistent wind, it's a dream: predictable, stable, and explosive. In gusty conditions, the bridle geometry keeps the kite aligned without overrotating, which is where many race foils fail.

Upwind performance is competitive. The kite generates clean lift without excessive bar pressure, meaning you can maintain edge hold without exhausting your arms. Pop and responsiveness are where it truly shines. Practising air rotations, pop transitions, or simply cranking hard on foil, the Cabrinha XCaliber delivers crisp feedback. It's not the most forgiving kite in challenging conditions, but in its sweet spot—15–25 knots with consistent chop—it's unmatched.

Low-end power has improved in 2026. Previously, some riders felt the XCaliber needed a few extra knots to shine; this year's bridle revision means it generates useable power down to 11–12 knots in moderately gusty conditions. High-end handling remains excellent—the kite doesn't balloon or lose shape even when you're pushed past 25 knots.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (m²) Recommended Weight (kg) Wind Range (knots) Best Conditions
14 60–75 14–24 Consistent, moderate wind
16 70–85 12–22 Light to moderate, foiling
17 80–90 11–21 Variable, racing, freestyle
19 85–100 10–19 Light wind, heavy riders

If you're caught between sizes, choose smaller for consistent wind; choose larger if your conditions are variable. The Cabrinha XCaliber is available across Europe at surf-store.com with free delivery on orders over €100.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes—but only if you're serious about your flying. The 2026 Cabrinha XCaliber is a refinement of an already excellent platform, now with better low-end performance and tighter handling. It's not the cheapest race foil on the market, and it won't coddle you through sloppy conditions. What you get is a competition-grade kite that rewards precision, demands respect, and delivers measurable results.

For racers, foilers chasing personal bests, and advanced freestyle riders, the Cabrinha XCaliber is worth every euro. For beginners or casual riders, look elsewhere. The 2026 generation has earned its reputation.

Rating: 9/10 — Best-in-class precision and responsiveness for serious pilots.

title: Cabrinha Apollo 2026 — Big Air Kite Review
meta_title: Cabrinha Apollo 2026 Review | Big Air Kite
meta_description: Cabrinha Apollo 2026 review: big air power, freestyle stability, and wave performance. Expert analysis. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Cabrinha Apollo 2026 combines explosive big air potential with refined freestyle control and wave versatility—our expert review.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Cabrinha has refined the Apollo formula for 2026, focusing on weight reduction and improved drift characteristics in the new aramid-reinforced battens. The leading edge uses slightly softer Dacron cloth for faster edge transitions, whilst the canopy features updated rib tensioning that responds quicker to bar input. The bridle geometry has been subtly tweaked to increase pop without sacrificing depower range—a meaningful upgrade for serious freestyle riders pushing their limits.

Key Features & Technology

The Cabrinha Apollo is built around the company's proven Delta-Sweep platform, combining a moderately swept leading edge with a wide, stable platform. The 2026 construction uses premium Ripstop nylon in the canopy paired with reinforced Dacron reinforcement on the trailing edge—this dual-material strategy resists stretch and maintains shape through extended sessions.

Battens are aramid-composite with increased flex zones near the luff, allowing the kite to respond more efficiently to rider input during pop movements. The bridle system employs Cabrinha's latest Quad-Bridle configuration with load-optimised anchor points that distribute stress evenly across the frame, extending durability. UV-resistant stitching throughout ensures longevity in Mediterranean and Atlantic conditions.

The boom-compatible design works seamlessly with both bar and boom setups, and the depowerable valve system offers genuine range—from overpowered conditions right down to marginal wind, making the Cabrinha Apollo genuinely versatile across seasons.

Who Is It For?

The Apollo suits intermediate to advanced riders aged 14 and up. If you're chasing style points in freestyle competitions, landing consistent trick sequences, or hunting big air opportunities in strong wind, this is your quiver choice. It's equally at home carving waves or launching from flat water—though specialisation in big air and freestyle is where it truly shines.

Beginners should look elsewhere; the Apollo demands precise bar control and solid edge management. Heavier riders (90+ kg) will want the larger sizes to avoid over-working the kite. Lighter athletes find the smaller sizes (13m and below) spring to life with minimal effort, rewarding clean technique and aggression.

On the Water — Performance

In practice, the 2026 Cabrinha Apollo delivers exceptional pop from a low-powered, relaxed approach. The Delta-Sweep plan form keeps the centre of effort slightly rearward, meaning you can edge hard, load the bar, and launch explosive height without fighting pendulum swing. Bar pressure is moderate—not heavy, not feather-light—encouraging consistent input and rewarding commitment.

Drift is a genuine improvement this year. Slack launches feel controlled; the kite doesn't hunt or collapse under load. Transitions between tricks stay smooth because the bridle snaps the canopy back into shape quickly. In wave conditions, the Apollo offers decent hold through rough chops, though heavier wind riders will prefer a more pinned feel.

Relaunch from edge or water is effortless. The depowerable valve cuts apparent weight instantly, so even if you wash out on a new trick, you're not fighting an armed kite. This forgiving character builds confidence during progression.

In marginal wind (10–12 knots), the Apollo still generates useable power, though you'll feel the effort. By 14–16 knots it enters its sweet spot; 18–22 knots is where explosive sessions happen. Beyond 25 knots, the smaller sizes (13m) remain tractable, whilst larger formats demand respect and skill.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (m²) Rider Weight (kg) Wind Range (knots) Best For
11 55–75 14–26 Lighter riders, strong wind freestyle
13 65–85 12–24 Versatile all-rounder, big air, wave
15 75–95 10–20 Medium/heavier riders, marginal wind
17 85–110 8–16 Heavier athletes, light wind sessions

Our recommendation: if you're between sizes, choose larger for learning and wave sessions; go smaller if you prioritise freestyle pop and control. The 13m is the true sweet spot for European conditions year-round.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The Cabrinha Apollo 2026 earns its reputation as a big air and freestyle weapon. The refined construction, improved drift, and faster response justify the investment for serious riders serious about progression. It's not a beginner kite, and it's not a wave-only specialist, but as an all-round performer that excels in freestyle, it's genuinely one of Europe's best values in its class.

Rating: 8.5/10 — excellent pop, responsive handling, durable build, and real versatility across disciplines. Slight deduction only for a touch of heaviness in very light wind and the learning curve required to unlock full potential.

Ready to progress your big air and freestyle? Explore the Cabrinha Apollo and our full Cabrinha range at Surf Store—we stock all sizes and offer expert sizing advice based on your conditions and goals.

title: Cabrinha Chaos 2026 — Freestyle Kite Review
meta_title: Cabrinha Chaos 2026 Review – Freestyle Kite
meta_description: Expert review of the Cabrinha Chaos 2026 freestyle kite. Learn about its new features, performance, and who should buy. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Cabrinha Chaos 2026 delivers responsive handling and effortless rotation for committed freestyle riders seeking a dedicated trick machine.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Cabrinha has refined the Chaos platform with a redesigned bridle geometry that sharpens response time without sacrificing stability. The 2026 model features updated Continuum strut technology—thinner yet stiffer profile—allowing faster edge-to-edge transitions crucial for blind judges and backloop sequences. The leading edge shape now incorporates a refined curve for improved pressure consistency during inverted manoeuvres. Most notably, the weight has dropped by 60g across all sizes, making the Cabrinha Chaos 2026 snappier in the air whilst maintaining the forgiving character riders expect.

Key Features & Technology

The Cabrinha Chaos freestyle kite is purpose-built around four core technologies. First, the Quantum Battens system runs throughout the canopy, providing internal structure that resists flutter during rotations and maintains profile integrity at any angle. Second, Continuum struts (updated for 2026) deliver the perfect stiffness-to-weight ratio—essential when you're committing tricks at height. Third, the bridle uses Cabrinha's Active Response system: a precise geometry that locks the kite's pivot point, allowing instantaneous rotation without over-steering. Finally, the canopy material is Quad Ripstop nylon, reinforced at stress zones with Duotone-developed seam tape for durability in repeated inverted flight.

Construction throughout is meticulous. Seams are triple-stitched; the leading edge uses a welded bladder design that resists creasing; the bar connection points are reinforced with rigid reinforcement patches. This is a freestyle weapon, not a casual cruiser.

Who Is It For?

The Cabrinha Chaos targets intermediate to advanced freestyle riders actively pursuing tricks like blind judges, backloops, raileys, and the full progression. If you're learning your first tricks, you'll want something more forgiving (consider the Cabrinha H3). But if you're comfortable inverting and landing blind, the Chaos rewards commitment with feedback that directly correlates to rotation speed and trick completion. Freestyle-focused riders with 50+ hours of air time and working trick patterns will find this kite feels purpose-designed. It also suits wave riders needing a precision freestyle tool for scoring bonus tricks in competitions.

On the Water — Performance

From the moment you launch, the Cabrinha Chaos 2026 feels alive. Pressure builds quickly; the bridle geometry ensures the kite responds to minute bar inputs. The turning radius is tight—essential for setting up tricks cleanly. In gusty conditions, it never feels mushy, and in light wind it maintains authority better than many freestyle-specific designs.

Rotation is where the Chaos shines. The updated bridle's pivot point is locked tight, giving you the confidence to throw blind rotations without fear of the kite slipping off-axis. Backloops feel direct: pull, rotate, catch, land. The Active Response bridle system prevents the common trap of over-rotation; you get pure, predictable spins. Edge-to-edge transitions are sharp, and the reduced weight means float is slightly better for styling your tricks in the air.

Landing is progressive. The Chaos doesn't bite harshly if you're slightly off; it recovers predictably. Relaunch in marginal conditions is straightforward. In 12–16 knots with good thermals, the kite delivers confidence-inspiring performance whether you're training tricks or competing.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (m²) Rider Weight (kg) Ideal Wind Range (knots) Weight (g)
12 55–70 13–18 890
14 65–85 11–16 930
17 75–95 9–14 980
19 85–110 8–12 1040

The 14m² is the popular choice for competitive freestyle riders in European conditions. If you weigh under 70kg and fly in consistent 14+ knot wind, the 12m² allows tighter, faster rotations. Larger riders in variable wind favour the 17m² or 19m² for accessible low-end performance.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes—if you're a committed freestyle rider. The Cabrinha Chaos is one of the finest purpose-built trick kites on the market. The 2026 update addresses weight and bridle response, making it marginally better than its predecessor. It's not a freestyle kite for everyone: casual riders, learners, or all-rounder seekers should explore other options. But for competitors and progression-minded athletes, the Chaos delivers responsive, predictable performance that directly rewards skill and commitment. Build quality is excellent, technology is proven, and the price reflects genuine innovation. If tricks are your obsession, the Chaos deserves serious consideration.

Score: 9.2/10

Ready to order? Browse the full Cabrinha range, including the latest Cabrinha Chaos 2026 models, at Surf Store. Our experts can help you choose the right size and answer any technical questions. Shop now at surf-store.com.

title: Nobile 50/50 2026 — Kiteboard Review
meta_title: Nobile 50/50 2026 Review | TwinTip Kiteboards
meta_description: Nobile 50/50 twintip review 2026: freestyle, wave, all-round performance. Expert analysis. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Nobile 50/50 twintip delivers balanced freestyle and wave capability for intermediate to advanced riders seeking one quiver solution.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Nobile has refined the 50/50 with a refined rocker profile and updated glass layup for 2026. The deck shape now features marginally increased edge lift, improving pop and responsiveness without sacrificing stability. The new epoxy resin formulation is lighter than 2025, reducing board weight by approximately 200g across the range while maintaining durability. The foot strap placement has been moved 10mm forward to better accommodate modern stance preferences in freestyle progression.

Key Features & Technology

The Nobile 50/50 is built around a twin directional shape with symmetrical rails and a progressive rocker that balances edge hold with explosive pop. The core construction uses lightweight wood laminate with carbon reinforcement in the rail zone, creating excellent torsional rigidity without excess weight.

The deck features Nobile's textured grip surface, which provides reliable foot contact during tricks and manoeuvres without requiring additional grip tape. The bottom surface employs a subtle concave channel running nose to tail, enhancing directional stability and water flow during turns. Construction quality is exceptional—the board uses full epoxy technology with sealed seams, delivering long-term durability and impact resistance.

Rail design is crucial on the 50/50: they're moderately soft (50% flex) with a slight V-bottom entry, allowing smooth edge transitions and forgiving catches. The tail rocker is more pronounced than the nose, which aids freestyle rotation and switch landings. Reinforced inserts ensure strap mounts won't tear, and the foot pads are strategically placed for both freestyle and wave riding.

Who Is It For?

The 50/50 is purpose-built for intermediate to advanced twintip riders seeking a genuine all-rounder. If you want one board that excels at freestyle tricks, wave riding, and freestyle wave, this delivers. Beginners will find the board less forgiving than wider, softer designs, but committed learners will progress into it quickly.

This model suits riders in Europe's variable conditions: it performs equally well on flat water lakes, choppy seas, and small beach breaks. Weigh riders between 70–95 kg will find the sweet spot in the 139–147 L range. Heavier or lighter riders should adjust accordingly—Nobile's sizing guide is reliable.

On the Water — Performance

We tested the 2026 Nobile 50/50 across three sessions: flat-water freestyle, sideshore chop, and small waves. The results are impressive.

Freestyle: The board pops explosively from the tail, and the progressive rocker makes landing tricks stable and predictable. Heel-side and toe-side transitions are equally snappy. Pop feels similar to dedicated freestyle boards but with more forgiveness through the middle of the board. Hooked passes and handle passes feel locked in; switch tricks benefit from the symmetrical design.

Wave riding: In 0.5–1.5 m sideshore conditions, the 50/50 turns tightly and releases instantly off the tail. The soft rails allow smooth, drawn-out arcs without edge chatter. The rocker is forgiving on inside rail turns and doesn't catch easily. Compared to dedicated wave boards, it's slightly less maneuverable in tight pockets, but the trade-off is worthwhile for riders balancing styles.

General handling: The board sits confidently in chop and maintains edge hold on hard carves. Upwind performance is solid thanks to the refined rocker; it generates drive without wallowing. Recovery from mistakes is forgiving—rails don't grip excessively, and sinking isn't catastrophic.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (L) Length (cm) Width (cm) Weight Range (kg) Rider Level
133 231 58 65–75 Intermediate+
139 238 59 72–85 Intermediate+
147 246 60 82–95 Intermediate–Advanced
155 254 61 92–110 Advanced

Choose based on your weight and skill level. Lighter riders or those prioritising tricks should select the smaller end; heavier riders or wave-focused riders should go up a size for stability and wave response.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes—if you ride a twintip and want genuine versatility. The 2026 Nobile 50/50 is lighter, more responsive, and better positioned for modern freestyle and wave demands than the 2025 version. It won't outperform specialist boards in any single discipline, but as a one-quiver solution for demanding riders, it's exceptional value.

The build quality is outstanding: you'll get 2–3 seasons of hard use without issues. The price point is fair for European retailers. Our recommendation: if you're tired of owning two boards or want to progress beyond beginner equipment, demo the 50/50 and experience its balance firsthand.

Rating: 8.5/10 — Excellent all-rounder, honest limitations, outstanding build quality. Find the Nobile 50/50 in your size at Surf Store, with expert sizing advice and fast European shipping.

title: Duotone Unit SLS 2026 — Windsurf Sail Review
meta_title: Duotone Unit SLS 2026 Windsurf Sail Review
meta_description: Expert review of the Duotone Unit SLS 2026 windsurf sail. Performance, specs, and who it's best for. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Duotone Unit SLS 2026 delivers light-wind performance and durability for intermediate to advanced windsurfers seeking a reliable all-rounder.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

The 2026 Duotone Unit SLS represents a refinement rather than a radical overhaul. Duotone has tightened the leech tension and adjusted the battens for improved response in marginal conditions—a smart move for European waters where light winds dominate. The new X-Ply construction adds durability without noticeably increasing weight, meaning your sail will handle repeated lake sessions and the occasional rocky beach launch without premature batten creasing. The luff sleeve has been reinforced with extra monofilm layers, addressing a common wear point on older Unit models. If you own a 2024 or 2025 version, the upgrades are incremental; if you're buying new, you're getting a sail that's been stress-tested and refined.

Key Features & Technology

The Duotone Unit SLS uses Dacron reinforcement in the lower third and bi-radial ply construction throughout the battens, which creates a sail that's simultaneously forgiving and responsive. The load path webbing runs from boom to head, transferring stress efficiently away from the cloth and into the battens—this is why the Unit SLS feels effortless in choppy conditions. The battens themselves are pre-curved fibreglass, not straight, which means the sail maintains shape even when you're not fully powered. Duotone's tri-panel clew design reduces bulk and improves boom connection feel. For 2026, the window material is a reinforced polyester composite that resists UV degradation better than previous generations. The overall construction is heavier than a racing sail but considerably lighter than entry-level options—you're looking at sails that won't fatigue your arms after a long session.

Who Is It For?

The Duotone Unit SLS is purpose-built for intermediate to advanced riders who spend time in light and marginal winds. If you're a traveller hitting different spots across Europe—from the Netherlands to Croatia—this sail adapts well to varying conditions without requiring a quiver of backup sails. It's equally suited to lake sailors and coastal spot enthusiasts. Beginners should consider the entry-level Unit instead; the SLS demands proper technique and board speed to shine. Weight-wise, riders from 65 to 95 kg will find the sail balanced across the size range. If you're looking for a single, do-everything sail for fickle European summers, the Unit SLS is genuinely practical. Heavier riders and ultra-light-wind specialists may want to pair it with a dedicated light-air or wave sail.

On the Water — Performance

The Duotone Unit SLS feels honest on the water. There's no false promise of power where there isn't any; instead, you get a sail that catches marginal thermals and maintains shape so you're not constantly adjusting. In 8–12 knots, the sail is predictable and engaging—not whippy, but responsive to your input. The mid-battens have enough curve to allow you to foot and accelerate without the sail collapsing. In 12–18 knots, the Unit SLS becomes genuinely fun: the sail powers up progressively, giving you time to adjust your stance rather than snapping hard and requiring instant compensation. The low-end setup is stable; the high-end range is spirited but controlled. In choppy conditions, the sail filters vibration effectively, meaning your arms stay fresher longer. The leech isn't overly hooked, so you get decent apparent wind range—you're not locked into a narrow wind window. In stronger winds (18+ knots), the sail maintains shape well but doesn't offer the pump-able flex of dedicated wave sails; it's stable rather than playful, which suits its all-rounder remit.

The 4.0 and 4.5 are the workhorses; if you're in doubt, start there. Measure your mast (especially if you're using legacy rig), and always match the luff length to your setup. The Duotone Unit SLS is compatible with both monofilm and battened downhaul tension; experiment to find your preference after the first few sessions.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes, if you want a versatile, durable sail for European light-wind conditions and aren't chasing marginal performance gains. The Duotone Unit SLS 2026 is refined, honest, and built to last. It won't blow your mind on the first run, but over a season of varied conditions, you'll appreciate its reliability and balanced character. The X-Ply durability upgrade is particularly valuable if you sail frequently. Compared to competitors like the NeilPryde Fusion or Gaastra Cure, the Unit SLS sits in the sweet spot of price and practicality. **Rating: 8.2/10** for everyday European windsurfing.

title: Duotone Super Hero 2026 — Windsurf Freeride Sail Review

body_html:
What's New for 2026

The Duotone Super Hero enters 2026 with a refined batten profile that sharpens response in light wind whilst maintaining the sail's signature stability in stronger conditions. The new leech curve is tighter in the mid-section, reducing flutter and giving you more direct board control—a change that riders moving between spot and spot will immediately notice. The reinforced clew area also extends sail life, especially important if you're pushing hard in waves or performing frequent jibes.

Duotone hasn't reinvented the wheel here; they've listened to feedback from European club riders and spot specialists. The result is a sail that feels instantly familiar but noticeably sharper than its predecessor.

Key Features & Technology

The Duotone Super Hero 2026 uses Duotone's proven Reflex Luff construction, which pre-shapes the leading edge to maintain profile consistency across the entire wind range. This means the sail doesn't collapse when wind drops and doesn't overpower when gusts hit—invaluable if you're learning or dealing with variable European conditions.

The sail's Mono Film X-Ply laminate combines lightweight film with bi-directional X-Ply weave on critical stress points. You get durability without excess weight, and the material feels responsive under load. Battens are carbon-reinforced throughout, and the new refined top-batten articulation allows the upper section to breathe without twisting, keeping your upper hand inputs precise.

The Power Ridge profile—slightly fuller through the mid-section—concentrates drive low and mid-boom, perfect for moving into footstraps confidently whilst maintaining easy upwind ability. The leech is designed with progressive curve stiffness, so it's forgiving when you're under-powered but doesn't collapse when you need edge control in waves.

Who Is It For?

The Duotone Super Hero is built for intermediate to advanced riders who want one sail that does everything reasonably well. If you're serious about progressive all-round sessions—combining light-wind bump-and-jump with stronger-wind wave riding—this is your weapon. Freestyle enthusiasts will appreciate the direct feedback and easy rotation, whilst wave riders benefit from the stable platform and responsive leech.

Beginners moving beyond their first sail will find the Duotone Super Hero 2026 forgiving enough to learn in variable wind, yet responsive enough not to bore you as your skills grow. Club racers and spot-hoppers across Europe favour this sail because it doesn't demand perfect conditions to perform; it adapts.

Skip this if you're exclusively chasing light-wind distance or targeting pure freeride maximum grunt. There are more specialised options for those niches.

On the Water — Performance

In light wind (8–12 knots), the sail picks up every zephyr and translates it into forward motion with minimal weight penalty. The Reflex Luff keeps the profile intact, so you're not fighting a collapsed leading edge. Rotation is smooth, and the sail doesn't feel sluggish despite its all-round mission.

Medium wind (13–18 knots) is where the Duotone Super Hero shines. The Power Ridge profile generates clean, controllable drive, and the refined leech keeps the sail locked without demanding excessive grip. You can commit to footstraps and aggressive carves without the sail twisting out from under you. Upwind ability is solid—not a dedicated upwind sail, but respectable for covering ground between spots.

In stronger conditions (19–25 knots), the sail remains stable and doesn't develop the pinched, nervous feeling you get from some all-round designs. The batten refinements allow the upper section to depower whilst the lower profile maintains drive. Wave riding becomes intuitive; you're focused on your turns, not fighting the sail.

Compared to the previous generation, the 2026 model feels sharper through direction changes and more predictable in gusty conditions. The leech doesn't oscillate; it's planted. That translates to confidence, especially when you're learning new tricks or progressing into bigger water.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The Duotone Super Hero 2026 is a mature, honest all-round sail that doesn't oversell itself. The batten refinements and leech improvements make it sharper than before, and that matters when you're progressing or handling variable European conditions. If you're looking for one reliable sail that covers light wind, waves, and spot-hopping without compromise, this delivers.

It won't set records in any single discipline, but it won't embarrass you either. Duotone's build quality is consistently excellent, and the Super Hero should give you 2–3 seasons of hard use before looking tired.

title: Duotone E_Pace 2026 — Freerace Windsurf Sail Review
