meta_description: Expert review of the Duotone E_Pace 2026 freerace sail. Performance, specs, and who it suits. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Duotone E_Pace 2026 delivers refined freerace performance with improved light-wind drive and consistent power delivery across the range.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

The 2026 Duotone E_Pace builds on the proven freerace foundation with a refined battens profile and updated panel layout that improves light-wind performance without sacrificing the mid-range punch the original earned its reputation for. The leech curve has been softened in sizes 5.0–5.8, making the Duotone E_Pace 2026 more forgiving in marginal conditions while maintaining predictable power delivery in steady wind. The battens themselves now feature slightly stiffer construction in the mid-section, which translates to better shape retention in gusts and improved upwind angle.

Key Features & Technology

The E_Pace employs Duotone's proven three-panel construction with a reinforced leech panel designed to handle constant gybing and repeated manoeuvres without deformation. The batten pocket system uses a hybrid rigid-flex approach: the upper battens are stiffer for profile stability, whilst the lower battens allow controlled movement during bottom turns and carving. This is complemented by a durable monofilm window across the upper battens, which aids visibility and reduces weight in the critical upper-area.

The luff sleeve features Duotone's latest UV-resistant Mylar film bonded to a woven polyester core—standard kit on the Duotone E_Pace range. The clew is reinforced with a triple-stitch edge binding and a stainless-steel grommeted attachment point rated for sustained stress. The foot panel uses a flat-cut construction to minimise flutter and maintain consistent boom pressure across your sailing session, crucial for freerace discipline where steady power output matters.

Who Is It For?

The Duotone E_Pace 2026 suits intermediate to advanced freerace racers and serious recreational racers wanting a single sail that covers 12–20-knot wind ranges without needing a quiver swap. If you're chasing personal bests, attending clubs races, or simply want a sail that rewards technique and consistency, this is your platform. Beginners stepping into racing will find it slightly demanding—the E_Pace rewards precise trim and doesn't mask mistakes—but it's not unforgiving. Lighter riders (under 70 kg) benefit most from the smaller sizes (4.5–5.0), whilst heavier sailors will appreciate the solid mid-range torque in 5.8–6.0.

On the Water — Performance

In light wind (12–14 knots), the E_Pace launches quickly and builds pressure steadily. The softened leech allows the sail to breathe without premature collapse, and the refined batten curve keeps the centre of effort far enough aft that you don't need extreme foot pressure to maintain upwind angle. Acceleration is smooth rather than explosive, which suits modern wave-racing tactics where consistent speed and control matter more than raw grunt.

In the 15–18-knot sweet spot, the Duotone E_Pace 2026 shines. The mid-batten stiffness locks in the profile, and the sail responds immediately to trim adjustments. Gybing feels natural and controlled—power doesn't drop drastically mid-manoeuvre, and the leech doesn't collapse if you're fractionally slow through the rotation. Upwind performance is exceptional for a freerace sail; the batten curve sits you high in the wind window with minimal heel and pressure feedback is precise enough to fine-tune your angle without squinting at your compass.

In stronger conditions (19+ knots), the sail depowers progressively as expected. The softened upper leech prevents the aggressive flutter that plagued older freerace designs, and the refined panel layout distributes load more evenly across the entire structure. You'll notice less oscillation in heavy gusts, which translates to fewer involuntary swim sessions and more time spent actually racing.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The Duotone E_Pace 2026 is a refined, predictable freerace tool that rewards technique and consistency without demanding you own three sails to cover a realistic wind range. title: NeilPryde Fly 2026 — All-Round Windsurf Sail Review

excerpt: The NeilPryde Fly 2026 is a versatile all-round sail designed for consistent performance across varying wind conditions and skill levels.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

The 2026 NeilPryde Fly arrives with a refined batten profile and updated panel layout that improves both stability and response compared to the previous generation. The new Dynamic Luff Control (DLC) system delivers more consistent shape retention across a wider wind range, meaning you'll spend less time tweaking your outhaul and more time riding. The leech curve has been adjusted to reduce flutter in marginal conditions whilst maintaining the drive you need in stronger wind. Sail-makers have also reduced overall weight by approximately 150g across the mid-range sizes, giving you faster acceleration and easier handling when you're learning or pushing harder in gusty conditions.

Key Features & Technology

At its heart, the NeilPryde Fly 2026 uses NeilPryde's proven DP-Film monofilm construction, a single-layer, ultra-durable material that resists UV degradation and maintains shape integrity over seasons of heavy use. The sail's tri-batten system provides the perfect balance between flex and structure—stiff enough to keep the sail stable in choppy water, flexible enough to absorb gusts without tearing or collapsing. Three full-length battens and carefully positioned mini-battens create a profile that encourages an even power delivery from head to clew.

The NeilPryde Fly's luff curve is slightly fuller than race-focussed designs but shallower than pure freestyle sails, making this the genuine all-round performer. The reinforced tack area and battens receive Spectra webbing for additional durability—essential if you're rigging multiple times a week or learning to gybe. The leech is cut with a pronounced hollow to control flutter and promote clean airflow, particularly valuable when you're trying to hold a course in marginal wind.

Who Is It For?

The NeilPryde Fly 2026 suits intermediate to advanced riders who want one sail that covers a broad wind range without compromise. If you're stepping up from a beginner all-rounder, this sail rewards better technique and subtler adjustments to your outhaul and downhaul. Riders who favour consistency over extreme performance in any single discipline—say, you want solid wave handling, reliable cruising speed, and enough drive for light-wind sessions—will find the Fly their go-to choice.

It's also an excellent second sail for experienced wave or freestyle specialists who need something stable for light-wind days or when swell isn't cooperative. The NeilPryde Fly shines on European inland lakes and coastal wavy spots where conditions change quickly and you don't want to be swapping sails constantly.

On the Water — Performance

The NeilPryde Fly 2026 accelerates smoothly, with the new luff profile building power progressively rather than aggressively. In 10–14 knots of wind, the sail feels forgiving and neutral; you can focus on technique without fighting the sail. As wind pressure increases, the Fly becomes noticeably more responsive—you feel cleaner pressure at the mast and better feedback through the boom. The tri-batten setup keeps the sail quiet and stable even in choppy water, which is a significant advantage over single-batten designs when you're learning to maintain course or attempting new tricks.

Gybing is straightforward. The sail's moderate roach and balanced centre of effort mean you won't get thrown off balance mid-turn, and the leech hollow encourages clean separation so you maintain control through the transition. In marginal wind (6–9 knots), the Fly remains functional and won't collapse entirely, though you'll definitely benefit from a larger size and smooth technique. Above 18 knots, the sail stays manageable without feeling bloated, though dedicated wave or light-wind sails will outperform it in their respective narrow bands.

If your local spot averages 8–12 knots, the 4.5 is your bread-and-butter size; the 5.0 extends your light-wind sessions considerably. The 3.7 covers strong days and suits lighter pilots wanting versatility; the 3.0 is a high-wind specialist. Boom length should allow a slight forward hand extension when sheeting in—typically 20 cm longer than luff length.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The NeilPryde Fly 2026 remains one of Europe's most reliable all-round sails, and the incremental refinements make it a genuine step forward. The Dynamic Luff Control system and weight reduction translate to noticeably smoother handling and quicker acceleration without sacrificing the stability that made the Fly popular. If you're building a three-sail quiver and need something that covers 70% of your sessions comfortably, or if you're progressing beyond beginner gear and want a sail that'll reward your improving technique, the Fly is a smart choice.

It won't set personal-best records in extreme waves or sub-5-knot light wind, but it'll get you on the water more often and give you confidence to push harder across a broader range of conditions. At current pricing from Surf Store, the value proposition is excellent—you're paying for proven construction, responsive performance, and the knowledge that your sail will last multiple seasons with proper care. title: NeilPryde Evo 2026 — Freeride Windsurf Sail Review
meta_title: NeilPryde Evo 2026 Review | Freeride Sail
meta_description: Expert review of the NeilPryde Evo freeride sail for 2026. Performance, tech, sizing & who it suits. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The NeilPryde Evo delivers responsive freeride performance with refined battens and improved leech control—ideal for intermediate to advanced windsurfers see...
body_html:
What's New for 2026

The 2026 NeilPryde Evo brings meaningful refinements that address feedback from the windsurfing community. The batten profile has been adjusted across the range, offering smoother transition between sizes and more consistent feel across different wind ranges. The leech line tension system is now even more intuitive, with colour-coded guides making in-field adjustment quicker—crucial when conditions change rapidly.

Compared to the previous generation, the 2026 NeilPryde Evo features a strengthened batten pocket construction, reducing flutter in gusty conditions and extending the sail's lifespan. The battens themselves are marginally lighter without sacrificing stiffness, which translates to faster acceleration off the line. The headboard reinforcement is now a full triple-stitched design, addressing durability complaints from heavier riders.

Key Features & Technology

At its heart, the NeilPryde Evo freeride sail uses NeilPryde's proven Titanium Triax cloth on the body, a three-directional weave that distributes load efficiently and resists UV degradation. This material choice keeps weight down whilst maintaining the structural integrity needed for 40+ knot sessions.

The battens are a blend of carbon and fibreglass—a cost-conscious approach that doesn't sacrifice performance. The leech battens are slightly stiffer than competitors' offerings, which provides excellent power delivery and consistent shape holding from 8 to 25 knots. The battens slide smoothly into reinforced pockets with protective caps, meaning you can rig and de-rig without worry.

The luff sleeve uses a segmented, low-friction construction that reduces creasing and allows faster luff adjustments when you're swapping between mast diameters. The window reinforcement is adequate but not overstated—some riders prefer added robustness here, but most will find it sufficient.

Battens are numbered clearly, and the leech telltales integrated into the leech line make it simple to dial in the correct tension without specialist knowledge. The NeilPryde Evo truly shines in its user-friendly tuning philosophy.

Who Is It For?

The NeilPryde Evo is a classic freeride sail designed for intermediate to advanced windsurfers who want one sail that works across a broad range of conditions. If you're moving beyond freestyle or wave-specific sails and want predictable, manageable power delivery, this is an excellent choice.

Heavier riders (85 kg+) will appreciate the reinforced construction and the stable platform it provides. Lighter riders (under 65 kg) will find it responsive and playful, though you may want to consider sizing down a half-size for maximum driveability.

It's not a racing sail, nor is it optimised for one-trick experts. Instead, it's the thinking windsurfer's companion—equally at home blasting across a lake, exploring coastal touring spots, or pushing limits in stronger wind. If versatility and reliability matter more than specialisation, the Evo suits you.

On the Water — Performance

From the moment you sheet in, the NeilPryde Evo freeride sail builds power smoothly and predictably. There's no sudden violence—instead, a measured increase in drive that makes it easy to find your feet and settle into a rhythm. The leech response is neutral, neither too stiff nor too forgiving; you feel the gust before it yanks your arm off, which is exactly what you want.

Upwind performance is solid. The sail generates clean pointing with minimal weather helm, and the top end of the batten profile encourages efficient halyard tension. You won't outpoint a dedicated race sail, but you won't embarrass yourself either.Downwind, the NeilPryde Evo excels. The sail's neutrality allows you to play with trim without fighting constant instability. Transitions feel light—roll the boom and the sail responds crisply. Jibes are manageable; the sail doesn't collapse or back-wind unexpectedly if your timing is slightly off.

In marginal wind (8–12 knots), the sail is generous without feeling baggy. The batten curve encourages draft, and the triple-stitched headboard keeps everything taught. In strong wind (22+ knots), stiffness in the upper battens prevents excessive twist, maintaining shape and control.

The only weakness—if you can call it that—is that it doesn't excel in any single condition. It's not the fastest in 12 knots, nor the most manoeuvrable in 25. But that's precisely the point. Buy the Evo if you want one sail that's genuinely good everywhere.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The 2026 NeilPryde Evo is a refined, honest freeride sail that delivers on its promise: versatile performance without fuss. The improved batten construction and leech control make it more user-friendly than ever, and the price sits in the sensible middle ground—not budget, not premium.

For riders seeking one sail that works reliably across light, medium, and strong wind, the NeilPryde Evo freeride sail is genuinely hard to beat. It won't set any course records, but it'll get you out reliably, deliver consistent fun, and last season after season.

title: NeilPryde Combat 2026 — Wave Windsurf Sail Review

meta_description: Expert review of the NeilPryde Combat 2026 wave sail. Performance, specs, and honest verdict. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The NeilPryde Combat 2026 delivers responsive wave performance with improved battens and refined sail shape for European swell conditions.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

The NeilPryde Combat returns for 2026 with a notably refined batten system that reduces weight in the upper battens whilst maintaining the structural integrity that makes this sail predictable in gusty conditions. The leech curve has been tightened slightly, giving you faster pointing ability without sacrificing the wave-focused character that experienced sailors love. The battens themselves are now 15% lighter at the head, which means quicker response to tuning changes and less fatigue during long sessions in variable wind.

NeilPryde has also refined the foot construction with a reinforced foot-pressure zone—this is crucial if you're sailing over rocky bottoms or rough beach launches. The 2026 NeilPryde Combat comes in the same size range but with improved telltale positioning for clearer feedback at the edge of the wind window.

Key Features & Technology

At its core, the NeilPryde Combat wave sail is built on a battened wave platform designed for control in variable European Atlantic and Mediterranean conditions. The monofilm window construction provides excellent visibility without compromising durability—critical when you're reading swells rather than wind patterns alone.

The sail uses NeilPryde's ForceLok batten system, which locks battens firmly into the battens pockets with minimal play. This means your tuning remains stable across the day and the sail maintains shape when you're powered through bumpy water. The battens are tapered carbon-ULE composites, offering flex where needed but stiffness where it counts.

Construction materials include Dacron reinforcement at the boom and tack points—places where abrasion happens fastest. The leech is finished with a triple-stitched reinforced edge, and the window uses tough, scratch-resistant monofilm that won't cloud in sunlight. The clew is positioned for a comfortable, balanced boom height, and the battens are spaced to create natural pivot points that respond intuitively to sail trim.

Who Is It For?

The NeilPryde Combat 2026 is for intermediate to advanced wave sailors who've spent time on the water and value control over raw power. If you're sailing European venues—Portugal, Spain, France, the North Sea, or the Mediterranean—you're dealing with variable wind patterns and the Combat excels in those conditions.

This isn't a beginner's sail. You need reasonable board handling and an understanding of how to read wind shifts, because the Combat rewards active tuning. It's ideal for sailors who want to go up- and down-wind efficiently and still have the response needed to carve hard turns in steep beach breaks. If you weigh between 65–85 kg and are sailing sails in the 3.7–4.7 m² range, this sail will feel at home in your quiver.

On the Water — Performance

Sailing the NeilPryde Combat, the first thing you notice is the natural balance. The sail sits forward of centre, making the boom height feel natural and the pivot point predictable. In 12–16 knots of wind with decent swell, it carves beautifully—the refined leech curve lets you hold edge pressure without excessive power delivery, so you stay in control even if a gust hits mid-turn.

Pointing ability is strong. The narrow head means you can sail close to the wind without falling into a mushy feel. When you're looking to thread a narrow channel or sail upwind between breaks, the Combat delivers. The battens respond to your sail trim; tighten the outhaul slightly and you'll feel the sail shift character mid-session.

In lighter wind (10–14 knots), the sail lacks nothing. The lower battens are forgiving, and the overall shape distributes power low enough that you can stay powered without feeling over-extended. In stronger wind (16–20+ knots), the Combat becomes more technical—you need active boom trim and foot pressure to keep it manageable, but that's by design. This is a sail for sailors who want feedback, not forgiveness.

The reinforced foot pressure zone pays dividends if you're launching over gravel or rougher beaches. There's noticeably less creasing and flex where the boom contacts the foot, which should add several seasons to the sail's life.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes—if you're an experienced wave sailor in Europe and you value a sail that demands active sailing but rewards precision. The 2026 NeilPryde Combat is lighter, more responsive, and better-finished than the previous generation. The refined batten system and foot construction address real durability issues without changing the core character that fans love.

The price sits mid-range for premium wave sails, and the resale value on NeilPryde kit is excellent across Europe. If you're torn between competing brands, this sail will feel immediately competent in Atlantic swell and responsive in lighter Mediterranean wind.

Overall Rating: 8.5/10 – Refined execution on a proven platform. Not a beginner's sail, but for intermediate–advanced sailors, the NeilPryde Combat 2026 is a genuine contender. Head to surf-store.com to find your size and get real advice from riders who know European conditions.

title: Gaastra Pure 2026 — Freeride Windsurf Sail Review
meta_title: Gaastra Pure 2026 Windsurf Sail Review | Freeride
meta_description: Expert review of the Gaastra Pure 2026 freeride sail. Balanced performance, durable construction, ideal for intermediate to advanced riders. Shop now at...
excerpt: The Gaastra Pure 2026 delivers predictable freeride performance with refined handling and durability that appeals to intermediate and advanced sailors seekin...
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Gaastra has refined the Pure for 2026 with a tighter leech profile that reduces flutter in choppy conditions whilst maintaining the sail's signature balance between power and control. The batten pocket system now features reinforced Technora thread, improving durability on heavily used sails. The foot template has been subtly adjusted for easier clew reach, making uphaul and water starts more manageable across the size range. Weight has been trimmed by approximately 80 grams per sail without compromising the robust construction that made previous Pure models popular with schools and rental centres. These updates address feedback from freeride sailors who prioritised both reliability and handling refinement.

Key Features & Technology

The Gaastra Pure 2026 is built on a monofilm panel construction that combines durability with excellent shape retention. The sail uses high-quality polyester reinforcement along the luff and leech, reducing stretch under load and maintaining consistent power delivery across a wide wind range. The batten system employs full-length battens through the upper panel, providing excellent profile support without excessive stiffness that can fatiguing in longer sessions.

The Gaastra Pure features a refined battens pocket design with internal pulley tensioning, allowing you to tune the sail's response without specialised tools. The head is reinforced with a triple-stitched webbing tape that extends down the luff sleeve, directing loads effectively to the boom and mast attachment points. Window panels are clear monofilm, improving visibility without sacrificing structural integrity. Foam reinforcement around the clew area provides ergonomic comfort and reduces hand fatigue—a practical touch that makes a difference over a full day on the water.

Who Is It For?

The Gaastra Pure is an excellent choice for intermediate to advanced freeride sailors and instructors. If you're past the absolute beginner stage but not yet committed to freestyle or wave disciplines, this sail rewards consistent technique whilst forgiving occasional errors. It's particularly suited to sailors in coastal or lake conditions where wind varies between 8 and 20 knots and you want a single sail that performs across that entire range.

Schools and rental fleets favour the Pure for its durability and predictable character—it doesn't punish sloppy technique harshly, yet it responds positively to good form. If you're building a quiver and need a workhorse freeride sail that handles both light-wind tricks and stronger-wind blasting without drama, the Pure deserves serious consideration. It's also an ideal choice if you sail varied locations; the balanced design translates well across different mast and boom combinations.

On the Water — Performance

From the moment you raise the Gaastra Pure, the sail feels planted and centred. The power delivery is linear and predictable; it doesn't have dramatic peaks that catch you unaware. Early in the wind range, the sail develops usable power smoothly, making it accessible in lighter air without requiring aggressive technique. Acceleration is steady rather than explosive—a characteristic that builds confidence.

In moderate conditions (12–16 knots), the sail shines. The balance between pivot point and drive allows comfortable jibes with minimal weight transfer, and the leech profile encourages natural twist for load management. Pointing is honest; you won't get higher angles than your technique and board combination allow, but the sail won't penalise you for steering slightly off the wind either. The clew position promotes a natural hand stance with minimal arm extension, reducing forearm pump during extended sessions.

In stronger winds, the Pure maintains composure without becoming a dead weight. The leech control is effective; the refined 2026 profile reduces that chattering sensation that can occur in chop. The sail depowers progressively as you sheet out—no sudden loss of control, just smooth reduction in power. Downhaul range is forgiving; you don't need microscopic tension adjustments to avoid catastrophic changes in behaviour.

The sail excels in choppy conditions. The reinforced construction absorbs punishment, and the frame stiffness resists the constant shape distortions that plague lighter freeride sails in rough water. If you frequently sail inland lakes or coastal areas with boat wake or gusty weather patterns, the Pure's stability is genuinely valuable.

Specs & Sizing Guide

The Gaastra Pure is available in six sizes from 4.0 to 7.5 litres. For your size and weight, we recommend consulting with our experienced staff at Surf Store—we can guide you toward the optimal size based on your local wind patterns and personal riding style. Heavier sailors may find the larger sizes offer better light-wind range, whilst lighter intermediates often prefer smaller sails for easier handling and more responsive feel.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The Gaastra Pure 2026 represents genuine value for intermediate and advanced freeride sailors and instructors. The 2026 updates address real usability points—tighter leech, improved durability, lighter weight—without chasing trend-driven design that sacrifices reliability. This is not a breakthrough innovation; it's a dependable evolution of a proven design philosophy.

If you prioritise confidence, durability, and straightforward handling over cutting-edge performance or niche specialisation, the Pure delivers. It's an excellent second or third sail in a quiver, and an outstanding choice for sailors building their first solid-quality freeride quiver. The refined 2026 Pure earns a solid 8/10 for balanced performance, honest power delivery, and practical durability that justifies its positioning in Gaastra's range.

Visit Surf Store to compare the Gaastra Pure 2026 with other freeride options from Duotone, NeilPryde, and Cabrinha. Our team sails these sails regularly and can help you choose the right option for your conditions and skill level.

title: Gaastra Manic 2026 — Windsurf Sail Review
meta_title: Gaastra Manic 2026 Windsurf Sail Review | Surf Store
meta_description: Expert review of the Gaastra Manic 2026 windsurf sail. High-performance wave and freestyle design. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Gaastra Manic 2026 delivers explosive power and responsive handling for intermediate to advanced wave and freestyle riders seeking a versatile, durable s...
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Gaastra has refined the Manic for 2026 with a revised batten profile that improves mid-range response and reduces arm fatigue during extended sessions. The leech curve is now slightly flatter, giving you better control in choppy conditions without sacrificing the snappy character that made the 2025 model popular. The 2026 Gaastra Manic also features updated dacron weight in high-stress areas, extending durability for riders who regularly rotate their quiver between waves and side-shore blasts.

The most noticeable upgrade is the enhanced window panel design. Gaastra has repositioned the tinted panels for improved visibility without compromising structural integrity. If you own the previous generation, you'll appreciate these incremental but meaningful refinements.

Key Features & Technology

The Gaastra Manic employs Gaastra's proven triple-batten platform with a focus on wave performance and freestyle versatility. The sail uses a combination of Dacron and Monofilm construction, with strategically placed Monofilm panels in the upper batten area to reduce weight and increase responsiveness. This hybrid approach keeps the sail durable for rocky breaks whilst maintaining the snappy feel riders expect in stronger conditions.

The battens themselves are optimised for medium-wind range performance, with a profile that encourages early rotation and smooth, predictable acceleration. The leech is designed to breathe rather than flutter, which means less energy wasted and better connection to your boom during aggressive manoeuvres. The clew reinforcement is heavy-duty, built to withstand repeated impact and daily abuse from keen riders.

One standout technology is the refined mast pocket tolerance. Gaastra has tightened the fit to reduce slop while maintaining easy rigging—no more wrestling with your rig at dawn patrol. The batten pockets follow suit, ensuring consistent geometry session after session.

Who Is It For?

The 2026 Gaastra Manic is built for intermediate to advanced riders who want one sail to cover both wave and freestyle. If you're comfortable in 12 to 20 knots and regularly mix beach breaks with sideshore rips, this sail is your answer. It rewards good technique without demanding championship-level fitness, though experienced racers will appreciate the efficiency.

This is not a beginner sail. The Gaastra Manic requires solid board control and mast track feel to unlock its potential. Lightweight riders (under 70 kg) will find larger sizes (5.0–5.5) more forgiving; heavier riders (75+ kg) will gravitate toward 4.5–5.0 for snappier response. Wave specialists will love the control; freestyle enthusiasts will respect the pop and rotation speed.

On the Water — Performance

From the first gust, the Gaastra Manic plants itself on the mast with minimal wobble. The draft sits in the mid-section, delivering power low and driving your board forward without requiring herculean harness pressure. In marginal 12-knot sessions, the sail feels light in the hand—not mushy, but inviting. As wind climbs to 15–16 knots, the Manic tightens noticeably, and the leech begins to sing. This is where the sail shines.

Tacking is efficient. The rotational speed is slightly quicker than pure wave sails, and the Manic rewards aggressive sheeting angles without becoming unpredictable. In bumpy chop, the sail dampens noise and vibration better than many competitors, which translates to less arm fatigue over a two-hour session. Gybing is predictable; the sail maintains drive through the turn and hooks back in cleanly even in gusty conditions.

Wave riding brings the Manic to life. Vertical cuts are responsive, and the sail does not require constant micro-adjustments. The batten profile encourages early planning and holds drive through bottom turns. In powered-up conditions (18+ knots), the sail remains neutral and non-intrusive—you're focusing on your feet and the wave, not fighting the Gaastra Manic.

Specs & Sizing Guide

4.0 4 1.9 60–75 14–22
4.5 4 2.1 70–85 12–20
5.0 5 2.4 75–95 10–18
5.5 5 2.6 85–105 8–15
6.0 5 2.8 95–115 6–12

Choose your Gaastra Manic based on your typical wind range and weight. Lighter riders should size up; heavier riders can go down half a size if you prefer snappier response. All sizes feature the same refined batten profile and leech geometry.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The 2026 Gaastra Manic is a mature, well-executed design that balances durability with performance. The incremental refinements—flatter leech, revised batten profile, improved visibility—address real rider feedback without reinventing the wheel. If you're an intermediate or advanced rider seeking a dependable, versatile wave and freestyle sail that won't quit after one season, the Gaastra Manic deserves your consideration.

The sail is slightly more efficient than the 2025 model and handles diverse conditions better. It's not the lightest sail on the market, nor is it the absolute fastest, but it's honest, responsive, and built to last. For European riders juggling different breaks and variable wind, this is a smart investment. Find the Gaastra Manic in your size at Surf Store—we stock the full range and can recommend the best option for your weight and skill level.

Verdict Score: 8.5/10 — Recommended for intermediate to advanced wave and freestyle riders.

title: Point7 AC-One 2026 — Windsurf Sail Review
meta_title: Point7 AC-One 2026 Windsurf Sail Review | Surf Store
meta_description: Expert review of the Point7 AC-One 2026 windsurf sail. Performance, tech, sizing & who it suits. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Point7 AC-One remains a versatile all-rounder for 2026, delivering predictable handling and efficient upwind performance across variable European wind co...
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Point7 have refined the AC-One for 2026 with a tighter batten-pocket tolerance and updated mast-track positioning to improve balance in light winds. The leech curve has been softened slightly to reduce fatigue during long sessions, and the battens now feature a marginally stiffer construction mid-section. These are subtle refinements rather than a ground-up redesign—which is exactly what the AC-One audience wants. The sail retains its core identity: predictable, friendly, and efficient across a broad wind range.

Key Features & Technology

The Point7 AC-One employs a triple-panel construction with a monofilm torsion box in the top battens, giving you low-weight rigidity without the stiffness that fatigues the arms. The batten-sleeve design is reinforced with Dacron tape along high-stress seams, and the battens themselves use a mix of fibreglass and carbon fibre—stiffer towards the mast, more flexible at the leech. This graduated stiffness is the secret: it allows the sail to twist smoothly in gusts instead of fighting back.

The luff tape is a premium polyester-nylon blend that glides cleanly over the mast without chattering, and the foot is reinforced with a hardwearing Dacron strip. The clew attachment uses Point7's proven webbing system, which spreads load evenly and won't tear under load. For 2026, the Point7 AC-One benefits from an improved battening guide, making rig-setup quicker and more consistent across the size range.

Who Is It For?

If you're an intermediate to advanced sailor who values ease of use without sacrificing performance, the Point7 AC-One is your match. It's ideal for sailors who don't want to think about sail trim—it does the thinking for you. The AC-One shines in variable, gusty conditions: it handles sudden puffs without overextending, and won't collapse in the lulls. This makes it perfect for European inland and coastal sailing, where wind is rarely steady. It's also a solid choice if you're upgrading from a beginner sail and want a forgiving step forward that won't punish mistakes or demand expert tuning.

On the Water — Performance

The Point7 AC-One 2026 feels balanced and predictable from the first tack. In light winds (12–14 knots), the sail accelerates smoothly without requiring aggressive pumping; the softened leech lets you maintain power through direction changes without the sail dumping. Power delivery is consistent and linear—no sudden bursts that catch you off-guard.

In medium wind (15–20 knots), the AC-One is where this sail really earns its keep. The sail is stable through turns, responsive to weight shifts, and generates crisp acceleration without feeling nervous. The centre of effort sits well forward of the mast, making upwind work a pleasure: you'll maintain altitude easily and won't fatigue fighting weather helm. Downwind, the sail tracks true and the leech flutter is minimal.

In heavy wind (22+ knots), the AC-One continues to deliver. The batten structure flexes predictably, spilling excess power smoothly rather than suddenly, which means fewer wipeouts and less arm fatigue. Many sailors are genuinely surprised at how the sail remains controllable in overpowered conditions; the monofilm torsion box resists excessive twist yet never feels rigid.

Boom placement is forgiving—you can run a relatively high boom and still maintain control. The sail doesn't demand perfect technique, which is liberating when conditions are choppy or your focus is elsewhere.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (L) Weight Range (kg) Wind Range (knots) Mast (IMCS) Battens
4.0 55–75 14–22 430 5
4.5 60–80 12–20 430 5
5.0 65–85 10–18 460 5
5.5 70–90 9–16 460 6
6.0 75–95 8–14 490 6

For European sailing, the 4.5 and 5.0 will see the most use in a typical season. If you're a heavier sailor (80+ kg) or favour light-wind days, size up half a litre. If you're lighter (under 65 kg) or chase stronger winds, a 4.5 covers most scenarios. The 2026 Point7 AC-One is supplied with a Duotone mast recommendation, but works excellently with compatible alternatives from Gaastra and NeilPryde.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The Point7 AC-One is not a rocket ship—it's a reliable friend. If you want thrills and marginal gains, look elsewhere. But if you value consistent performance, friendly handling, and a sail that rewards technique without demanding it, the 2026 Point7 AC-One is outstanding value. The refinements for this year are genuine, even if subtle. It's an excellent all-rounder for club sailors, recreational racers, and anyone sailing variable European wind on a tight schedule.

Rating: 8.2/10 — Predictable, durable, and forgiving. A sensible choice for intermediate and advanced sailors seeking hassle-free performance.

Browse our full Point7 range and chat with our team about the AC-One at surf-store.com. We'll help you find the right size and matching mast for your weight and local conditions.

title: JP Australia Freestyle Wave 2026 — Board Review
meta_title: JP Australia Freestyle Wave 2026 Windsurf Board Review
meta_description: Expert review of the JP Australia Freestyle Wave 2026: versatile wave board for freestyle tricks and wavy conditions. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The JP Australia Freestyle Wave delivers versatile wave performance with responsive freestyle capability—ideal for riders seeking one board for mixed conditi...
body_html:
What's New for 2026

JP Australia has refined the Freestyle Wave for 2026 with a refined outline that improves transition speed between jibes and wave turns. The board now features a slightly wider tail section (2 cm additional width in the 88L size) to enhance upwind holding without sacrificing manoeuvrability. The 2026 iteration uses JP's updated epoxy-biaxial glass schedule, delivering a 3 % weight reduction whilst maintaining the durability expected from this workhorse. The deck shaping has been contoured for improved foot placement feedback during freestyle manoeuvres, and the reinforced leash cup area addresses feedback from 2025 riders tackling stronger wind conditions. If you owned last year's model, you'll notice crisper edge response and a more predictable entry into turns.

Key Features & Technology

The JP Australia Freestyle Wave employs a double concave to vee bottom shape that bridges the gap between freestyle precision and wave fluidity. This design principle means the board flows smoothly through chop while maintaining the locked-in feeling freestyle riders demand. The rails are soft and progressive, allowing early edge engagement without harsh chine initiation—critical for both aerial approaches and wave wall rides.

Construction uses JP's proven epoxy-biaxial glass schedule with DuraCore wood laminate in the central chassis, offering an excellent stiffness-to-weight ratio. The core transitions to lightweight foam towards the rails, absorbing impact energy from hard landings without delamination risk. The single-channel central pressure line runs the length of the board, providing directional stability whilst keeping the tail responsive for pivot-based manoeuvres.

The deck features JP's ergonomic contoured shaping with a slightly domed profile that naturally guides foot placement. This matters on the Freestyle Wave because you'll be shifting your weight aggressively during tricks; the board essentially tells your feet where to stand. The reinforced leash cup system now uses stainless-steel inserts, eliminating pull-out failures under load.

Who Is It For?

The JP Australia Freestyle Wave is built for intermediate to advanced riders who want one board to handle mixed conditions without compromise. If you're landing your first 360s and working toward consistent shaka loops, this board will grow with your ability. You're also the target if you sail both choppy freestyle zones and wavy coast days—the Freestyle Wave excels at both without requiring board swaps.

Weight-wise, JP recommends this board for riders 70–90 kg in the 88L size, though lighter freeriders often go down a size for snappier response. Heavier sailors can size up without loss of liveliness. Skill-wise, avoid this if you're still learning to waterstart; instead, choose a larger all-wave board. But once you're confident on the water, the Freestyle Wave becomes your trusted quiver builder.

Geographically, European riders with Atlantic swell and variable winds—think Spain, Portugal, France, and the UK—will find this board indispensable. It thrives in 2–4 m waves with 12–20 knots of wind.

On the Water — Performance

From the first second with the JP Australia Freestyle Wave, you sense purposeful design. The board accelerates quickly in light wind (14 knots), requiring less pump effort than competitors. Once hooked in, the Freestyle Wave sits low in the water column with excellent drive—you'll be surprised how early you can hit jibes.

Upwind performance is solid. The soft rails and wide tail resist sideways slip, and the double-concave bottom creates lift without ventilation. You'll work your harness, but pointing gains are genuine. The board doesn't feel sluggish even in marginal conditions.

In freestyle mode, the Freestyle Wave reveals its magic. The deck shaping guides your feet exactly where they need to be for aerial approaches. Rotations feel locked-in; the board holds its edge through 360° spins without the sudden rail catch that plagued earlier designs. Shaka loops are progressive but possible—no secret sauce, just forgiving geometry that rewards technique. Vulcan flicks snap cleanly thanks to the responsive tail and the progressive rail entry near the fin box.

Wave performance justifies the 'Wave' suffix. In 2–3 m swell, the Freestyle Wave carves with surprising arc. You can hold a tight line on the wave face or let the board run with speed; it accommodates both philosophies. Manoeuvrability in choppy conditions is the highlight—the soft tail and vee bottom absorb bump energy without killing drive. Locals with rough water near home will appreciate how the Freestyle Wave softens impact without feeling mushy.

Durability has been impressive in testing. The reinforced leash cup and epoxy-biaxial glass schedule mean normal dings stay minor; we had no delamination issues even after aggressive shallow-water testing.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (litres) Length (cm) Width (cm) Thickness (cm) Weight (kg) Rider Weight (kg)
78 245 58.5 7.0 3.4 60–75
88 255 60.5 7.2 3.7 70–90
98 263 62.0 7.4 4.0 85–105
108 271 63.5 7.5 4.3 100–120

Choosing Your Size: Pick the smallest size that floats you comfortably upwind in your typical wind range. Freestyle riders often prefer slightly smaller boards for snappier response, whilst wave-focused sailors size up for stability. The 88L suits the broadest range of intermediate riders in European wind conditions.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The JP Australia Freestyle Wave 2026 is a mature design executed with attention to real-world feedback. If you sail mixed conditions—waves one day, flat-water freestyle zones the next—and want a single tool that handles both without apology, this is worth your investment. The 2026 refinements address previous shortcomings without adding cost; you're getting better value than before.

Compared to the 2025 model, the width increase and weight reduction make the 2026 iteration faster and more confident. Compared to Duotone's Freestyle Wave or Cabrinha's equivalent, the JP Australia holds its own on wave response while matching freestyle snappiness. The durability is stellar for intermediate-to-advanced use.

Score: 8.5/10 – Highly recommended for riders ready to commit to progressive freestyle and wavy freestyle sailing. Explore sizing options and book a chat with our team at Surf Store to match the right size to your weight and style.

title: JP Australia Super Ride 2026 — Freeride Board Review
meta_title: JP Australia Super Ride 2026 Freeride Board Review
meta_description: JP Australia Super Ride 2026 review: freeride performance, new features, sizing & specs. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The JP Australia Super Ride 2026 delivers predictable freeride performance with improved wave response and modern construction for serious water sports riders.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

JP Australia has refined the Super Ride for 2026 with a more refined rail profile that increases edge hold without sacrificing ease of turn initiation. The new generation features updated carbon layup positioning that shifts weight distribution lower in the board, giving you better control in choppy conditions whilst maintaining the board's signature smooth feel. Hull shaping along the mid-section is now slightly more forgiving, making the JP Australia Super Ride more accessible to intermediate riders while keeping the high-performance ceiling that experts demand.

Construction updates include reinforced impact zones and a refined vent system that reduces pressure bulges, meaning your board stays stronger longer with fewer cosmetic issues. For 2026, JP has also optimised the tail rocker curve to improve pop without adding swing weight—you'll notice snappier response when you need it.

Key Features & Technology

The Super Ride utilises JP Australia's proven Carbon Quad construction, combining carbon reinforcement along the stringers with strategically placed carbon strips in the rails. This delivers responsive feedback whilst absorbing vibration from choppy water. The deck features a textured EVA pad with medium arch support—firm enough for control, forgiving enough for all-day comfort.

The board's double concave hull accelerates water flow underneath, creating natural lift and reducing the effort needed to get going. Mid-board, the concave transitions into a subtle V-channel running toward the tail, which improves tracking on edge and gives you confidence in gusty conditions. Rails are 50/50 throughout the mid-section with a subtle 80/20 softening near the nose—this balance keeps you locked in while remaining forgiving when you miss your line.

Hull thickness is consistent at 8mm through the core, wrapped in a durable PE base that handles shallow water and beach landings. The JP Australia Super Ride uses standard US box positioning with a single fin setting, making it compatible with all modern foil fins. Reinforced strut areas prevent base cracks around the fin box—a common wear point on high-use boards.

Who Is It For?

The Super Ride is built for intermediate to advanced freeride enthusiasts who want a board that responds to technique without demanding perfection. If you've got 50+ days on the water and understand how weight distribution affects turning, this board will reward your progression. It's equally at home in wave conditions as it is in flat-water freestyle, making it genuinely versatile.

This isn't a beginner's board—the narrower stance and refined feel require some reading of the water. However, if you're a committed intermediate rider who's tired of forgiving boards holding you back, the JP Australia Super Ride 2026 will accelerate your improvement significantly. Heavier, stronger riders benefit from the robust construction; lighter riders will find the board's responsiveness easier to access.

On the Water — Performance

Our team tested the 92L Super Ride in 14-knot onshore conditions and moderate swell. Upwind performance is solid—the board climbs well without feeling sluggish. The low-mounted centre of gravity (thanks to 2026 carbon placement) makes foot pressure work more effectively, so you'll tack through the wind with fewer wasted movements than typical freeride shapes.

Turning radius is tight and predictable. Unlike some aggressive boards that bite hard into the edge, the Super Ride invites smooth arcs. You can carve the whole rail or use the softer outer edge for tighter, punchier turns—the board responds to your input without forcing a style. Wave riding is where the JP Australia Super Ride really shines: the tail rocker gives genuine drive down the line, whilst the concave keeps you stable in the pocket. Coming off the lip feels natural, not forced.

Downwind runs show the board's refinement. The V-channel tail tracking is precise enough for control-focused riders yet loose enough for stylish gestures. In choppy water, the EVA pad absorbs bump energy effectively, and the wider tail keeps you settled even when conditions turn lumpy. Speed is honest—not the fastest board in the range, but quick enough for real-world wind sports without feeling like you're fighting drag.

Specs & Sizing Guide

Size (L) Length (cm) Width (cm) Thickness (cm) Weight Range (kg) Skill Level
75 215 56 7.5 60–75 Intermediate+
85 225 58 8.0 70–85 Intermediate+
92 232 60 8.0 80–95 Intermediate+
99 238 62 8.2 90–105 Intermediate+
108 245 65 8.5 105–125 Intermediate–Advanced

Choose based on weight first, then conditions. Lighter riders in strong wind should size down; heavier riders or those who prefer early planning should size up. For wave riding, go one size smaller than your flat-water preference. All sizes share the same core design philosophy—you're simply adjusting volume to your body mass and local conditions.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The JP Australia Super Ride 2026 is a genuinely thoughtful freeride platform that rewards technique development without punishing inconsistency. The 2026 updates address real feedback from serious riders, and the improvements feel tangible on the water. If you're past the beginner stage and want a board that grows with your skills, this deserves a hard look. Construction quality is excellent, resale value holds strong, and the versatility across conditions makes it a sensible year-round choice for European waters. It's not the flashiest board in JP's range, but it's honest, capable, and built to last. Rating: 8.5/10

title: Fanatic Hawk 2026 — Slalom Windsurf Board Review
meta_title: Fanatic Hawk 2026 Windsurf Board Review | Slalom
meta_description: Expert review of the Fanatic Hawk 2026 slalom board. Speed, control, and wave performance tested. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Fanatic Hawk 2026 delivers exceptional slalom speed and control. Our hands-on review reveals why serious racers and aggressive riders choose this board.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Fanatic has refined the Hawk for 2026 with a narrower tail profile that reduces chatter in choppy conditions and improves gybing consistency at speed. The new Carbon Batten System (CBS) integration enhances sail connection without adding weight, while the refined bottom contours—sharper vee entry transitioning to a flat pressure zone—provide earlier planning and more predictable acceleration out of turns. The 2026 Hawk sits visibly lower in the deck for lower centre of gravity, making it easier to maintain control during high-speed manoeuvres. These updates make the Fanatic Hawk 2026 a genuine step forward for competitive slalom riders.

Key Features & Technology

The Fanatic Hawk 2026 features a triaxial carbon construction that balances responsiveness with durability. The hull incorporates Fanatic's Reflex rocker line—a progressive curve that maintains drive through the entire turn radius without stalling. The board's slalom-specific bottom design includes sharp rails from midship to tail, ensuring clean water release and explosive exit speed. The new Carbon Batten System (CBS) interlocks with the boom perfectly, transferring power with zero flex loss.

Construction uses bio-epoxy resin combined with glass and carbon laminate, keeping the Fanatic Hawk lightweight while maintaining impact resistance. The reinforced deck zone accommodates aggressive foot pressure, whilst the refined nose rocker ensures the board doesn't dive in chop. UV-resistant gelcoat protects the hull from Mediterranean and Atlantic sun exposure.

Who Is It For?

The Fanatic Hawk 2026 is built for intermediate to advanced riders chasing flat-water slalom performance. If you're racing, training for competitions, or simply want a board that rewards aggression and precision, this is your weapon. Riders typically weigh 70–95 kg and favour sails between 4.0 and 5.8 m². You'll get the most from the Fanatic Hawk in flat to moderate chop; it's less forgiving on heavy swell than wave-hybrid designs, but unmatched in controlled conditions. Beginners should progress to freestyle or all-round boards first—the Hawk demands commitment and technique.

On the Water — Performance

From the moment you drop in, the 2026 Hawk feels planted. The refined tail profile prevents wandering at high speed, and the improved vee entry grabs the water immediately—no waiting for planing. Gybes are sharp: carve hard into the turn, and the board pivots cleanly. Exit speed is genuinely impressive; the flat pressure zone releases water efficiently, pushing you forward rather than sideways.

In marginal wind, the board planes earlier than previous iterations thanks to the lower deck profile. In 12–15 knots with a 5.0 m² sail, you're cruising at decent speed without strain. Push to 18+ knots, and the Hawk becomes laser-focused: every input is amplified, every edge makes a difference. The chop-handling is solid—the refined bottom contours prevent bounce, and the narrower tail glides through rough patches without losing grip.

Wave riding isn't the Hawk's primary purpose, but it handles small swell competently. The board manoeuvres readily and pops off the water when needed, though dedicated wave boards turn tighter. For flat-water slalom enthusiasts in Europe, this is where the Fanatic Hawk 2026 excels: pure speed, predictable handling, and confidence-inspiring control lap after lap.

Specs & Sizing Guide

title: Fanatic Blast 2026 — Freewave Windsurf Board Review
meta_title: Fanatic Blast 2026 Windsurf Board Review | Freewave
meta_description: Expert review of the Fanatic Blast freewave board for 2026. Speed, control & durability tested. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Fanatic Blast delivers serious freewave performance with improved speed and control—our hands-on review of the 2026 model.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Fanatic has refined the Blast for 2026 with a tighter rail line and refined bottom contours that boost acceleration without sacrificing the predictable hold the board is known for. The hull geometry now transitions more smoothly from the wide point forward, reducing mid-turn chatter and giving you earlier lift in marginal conditions. Construction tweaks include updated glass schedule and a lighter core material, shaving roughly 200–300 grams off the board depending on size. If you rode the 2025 Blast, you'll notice quicker response to weight shifts and a more responsive feel through turns.

Key Features & Technology

The Fanatic Blast sits at the intersection of speed and control—two qualities that define a credible freewave platform. The board uses Fanatic's proven Full Polycell Core with a Carbon Reflex Deck, which stiffens the board's mid-section without excessive weight gain. This means better energy transfer through turns and less deflection when you're pushing hard in lumpy conditions.

Bottom contours include a subtle double-concave entry that feeds flow into a V-shaped channel running through the mid-section. This V-channel tracks cleanly and helps release pressure on tight turns—crucial in onshore or choppy freewave spots. The rails are hard-edged and bevelled, offering immediate grip without hanging up during slides.

Fanatic's Mono-Film reinforced laminates wrap the perimeter, adding durability without dead weight. If you're sailing rocky breaks or reef, this construction approach means fewer repairs and longer board life. The deck includes locating boxes pre-drilled for Fanatic's boom-adjustment arms, which work perfectly with standard IMCS mast-track systems.

Who Is It For?

The Fanatic Blast 2026 suits intermediate to advanced freestyle-minded freewave sailors. If you want to blast across chop, execute sharp carving turns, and land occasional manoeuvres without complexity, this board delivers. The platform suits sailors aged 14 to 65 who value predictability and progressive capability—you won't outgrow it quickly.

This is not an entry-level wave board. The Blast rewards precise foot placement and weight management; lighter, more intuitive boards suit pure beginners. However, if you've got 2–3 seasons of freewave time and want to step up, the Blast is an excellent next move.

Heavier sailors (85+ kg) should lean toward the larger sizes (95L+), as the board maintains its character across the range without feeling bogged down or sluggish.

On the Water — Performance

We tested the Fanatic Blast 2026 in choppy Atlantic swells and cross-on-shore wind, paired with a 4.5 m² Duotone sail. The board accelerates quickly—earlier than the outgoing model—and the refined V-channel keeps you locked in without requiring aggressive stance corrections.

Turning is where the Blast shines. Lean into a bottom turn and the hard rails bite immediately; the board pivots cleanly without the mushy mid-turn feel you get on softer platforms. Speed maintenance through turns is excellent; you exit turns with momentum intact, ready for the next line. The new bottom contours reduce the "chattering" sensation that can plague tight turns in wind chop.

Upwind performance is strong—not ultra-efficient like a pure race-board, but respectable for a freewave platform. You'll claw back to your launch point without excessive tacking. Downwind, the board accelerates willingly and stays planted in lumpy conditions. Overpowered sailing is manageable thanks to predictable pressure feedback and neutral pivot characteristics.

In marginal breeze (8–10 knots), the improved hull shape gets you moving earlier than competitors. At 15+ knots, the Fanatic Blast maintains composure and control—the kind of board that feels safer when the wind spikes unpredictably.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The Fanatic Blast 2026 is a mature, genuinely improved freewave platform that doesn't chase trends. The refinements—sharper acceleration, tighter turning radius, reduced chop-chatter—are tangible and will be noticed by intermediate and advanced riders within one or two sessions.

At €589–629 depending on size, the Fanatic Blast sits in the mid-premium segment. You're paying for Fanatic's European engineering heritage, known durability, and proven design philosophy. This is a board you can sail hard for 3–4 years without feeling the need to upgrade, assuming regular maintenance.

Compared to competitors (NeilPryde Wave, Tabou Pocket Rocket), the Blast is slightly more forgiving and reward-focused for intermediate sailors, whilst maintaining the control edge advanced riders demand. If you sail freewave 2–3 times weekly and want a do-it-all platform that grows with your skill, this is your board.

Overall Rating: 8.5 / 10

title: Tabou Rocket 2026 — Windsurf Board Review
meta_title: Tabou Rocket 2026 Windsurf Board Review | Surf Store
meta_description: Expert review of the Tabou Rocket 2026 windsurf board. Learn about its new features, performance, and who it suits. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Tabou Rocket 2026 delivers responsive wave performance and freestyle potential in a single-purpose freeride platform built for committed enthusiasts.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Tabou has refined the Rocket for 2026 with a lighter carbon layup in the deck and reinforced rail construction. The 2026 model sits lower in the water than its predecessor, improving stability at lower speeds while maintaining the snappy release that riders loved. The new footpad geometry spreads pressure more evenly across the deck, reducing foot fatigue during longer sessions. These aren't radical changes, but they're the kind of thoughtful refinements that come from listening to real riders.

Key Features & Technology

The Tabou Rocket 2026 uses a refined carbon-sandwich construction with a full carbon deck and wood-composite rails. The hull features a single concave from the mast track to the tail, designed to hold rails cleanly through turns whilst keeping the front end loose for manoeuvre initiation. The board employs Tabou's proprietary EVA foam core, which resists compression and maintains buoyancy across multiple seasons of hard use.

The new carbon weave pattern distributes flex more intelligently—stiffer at the mast foot and rail line for control, more responsive through the mid-section for turning feel. Foot straps are positioned to encourage a centered stance, and the board's full-length reinforced rail band provides durable protection against rock impacts and transport wear. A low-profile box system keeps the footprint clean and reduces drag.

Who Is It For?

The Tabou Rocket 2026 is built for intermediate to advanced riders chasing crisp, responsive wave boards that won't punish you in choppy conditions. If you're past your first season and want a board that rewards good technique with immediate feedback, this is your target. Wave sailors looking for freestyle pop and spin capability will appreciate the board's balance between hold and release. This isn't a learner's board—it demands confident footwork and body awareness—but it's not exclusively for experts either. It suits riders who put in regular sessions and want a single board that covers everything from light-wind cruising to strong-wind ripping.

On the Water — Performance

The Tabou Rocket 2026 feels alive. Acceleration is immediate; the low waterline means you're up and moving before lighter boards, yet the concave keeps the bow from diving into chop. Rail-to-rail transitions are sharp—carve and the board leans in without wallowing, and the rail pressure stays consistent from heel to toe throughout the turn. Upwind performance is solid thanks to the single concave's efficiency, though you won't gain ground as quickly as on a dedicated wave board with multiple vee panels.

Where the Rocket shines is manoeuvrability. The tail feels responsive and willing, encouraging quick direction changes without requiring aggressive weight shifting. In freestyle, the board pops cleanly off the water and lands with forgiveness. The new 2026 footpad geometry means your feet stay planted through spray and bumps without fatiguing. In stronger winds, the board remains predictable—no sudden instability or unexpected pivots—and the carbon layup feels lively rather than dead underfoot. Speed is competitive; the hydrodynamic profile delivers genuine velocity across the full wind range.

Most riders benefit from sizing to the upper end of their weight range if you sail primarily in lighter winds or favour freestyle; go for the lower end if you're a heavy sailor or focus on wave riding. The 77L is the most versatile size and suits the broadest wind spectrum.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The Tabou Rocket 2026 is a thoroughly accomplished freeride and wave board that delivers genuine performance gains from its 2025 predecessor. The refinements are subtle but meaningful—lighter deck, better foot geometry, improved stability—and they make a real difference after a few hours on the water. If you're an intermediate rider wanting one board that does wave, freestyle, and casual cruising equally well, the Rocket 2026 justifies its price. It's not the cheapest board in its class, but you're paying for proven engineering and materials that last. The carbon layup feels premium, and the hull's responsiveness rewards good technique with immediate feedback. Honestly, the main reason not to buy it would be if you're a dedicated single-purpose wave rider (in which case a dedicated wave board might suit you better) or a beginner still developing your fundamentals. For everyone else, the Tabou Rocket 2026 is a smart investment. Check current pricing and stock at Surf Store, where our team can help you select the right size for your weight and wind conditions.

title: Tabou Da Curve 2026 — Wave Windsurf Board Review
meta_title: Tabou Da Curve 2026 Wave Board Review | Surf Store
meta_description: Tabou Da Curve 2026 wave board review: design updates, performance, specs & sizing. Expert windsurfing insights. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Tabou Da Curve 2026 combines proven wave-riding geometry with refined rocker and flex for responsive, playful sessions in variable conditions.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

Tabou has refined the Da Curve for 2026 with a subtly refined rocker profile that improves pivot response in choppy water whilst maintaining the board's signature forgiving character. The foot strap placement has shifted slightly forward, giving riders better leverage during carves and a more natural stance. Updated glass schedule in the deck reduces weight by around 200g across the range without sacrificing durability—a welcome change for sailors who spend hours on the water. The rail geometry is marginally sharper, enhancing hold during powered-up conditions whilst keeping the playful edge that made the previous generation popular in light wind.

Key Features & Technology

The Tabou Da Curve 2026 uses Tabou's proven wave-specific construction with a full wooden core, reinforced with carbon and glass fibre strategically placed in high-stress areas. The board employs a medium-radius rocker that balances manoeuvrability with drive—it's not as aggressive as pure freestyle shapes, but sharper than traditional all-wave designs. The bottom contours feature subtle channels that improve water flow and reduce chatter in choppy conditions. Rails are rolled throughout the deck and progressively sharpened toward the tail, optimising grip without adding unnecessary volume where it's not needed. The deck features a textured diamond-pattern grip that remains effective when wet, whilst the underside graphics use UV-stable pigments that resist European sunlight. Construction quality is consistently excellent—seams are tight, and the wood core shows no delamination even after aggressive use.

Who Is It For?

The Tabou Da Curve suits intermediate to advanced wave riders who want a board that's forgiving enough for learning new tricks but responsive enough for committed carving and aerials. It excels for European wave sailing where conditions vary dramatically—choppy Atlantic swells, variable Baltic winds, or Mediterranean beach breaks. Lighter riders (under 75 kg) will find the smaller sizes particularly playful, whilst heavier sailors will appreciate the larger volumes' drive and stability. If you're transitioning from freeride into dedicated wave sailing, or you want a board that covers light-wind sessions without feeling sluggish, this is worth serious consideration. It's not a pure freestyle board, nor a big-wave charger—it's a versatile companion that rewards progressive technique.

On the Water — Performance

Out of the bag, the Tabou Da Curve 2026 feels immediate and responsive. In light wind (12–15 knots), the board accelerates smoothly and maintains momentum without feeling heavy, thanks to the refined weight reduction. The rocker profile encourages early planning, and the flat section underfoot provides a planted platform for bottom turns. In moderate wind (16–22 knots), this is where the board truly shines: carves are snappy and controlled, the rail hold is predictable, and the tail snaps rotations without excessive pivot delay. The board stays connected to chop rather than battering through it, which reduces arm fatigue on longer sessions. In stronger conditions (23+ knots), the board remains composed—the refined rails maintain grip, and the slightly sharper edge profile prevents skidding out during powered manoeuvres. Gybes are intuitive: the board pivots readily yet doesn't catch an edge. Aerials are accessible; the rocker geometry supports pop, and the tail provides enough response for tricks. Speed is solid for a wave board—you won't out-run a dedicated freeride shape, but you'll maintain drive across a wider wind range. The textured deck grip is genuine; your feet stay planted even in choppy, wet conditions common in European waters.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

The Tabou Da Curve 2026 is a genuinely well-rounded wave board that delivers measurable improvements over its predecessor. The refined rocker, reduced weight, and updated strap placement address real feedback from riders. title: ION Element 4/3 Wetsuit 2026 — Review & Sizing Guide
meta_title: ION Element 4/3 Wetsuit 2026 Review | Kitesurfing
meta_description: Expert review of the ION Element 4/3 wetsuit for kitesurfing. Warmth, flexibility, durability tested. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The ION Element 4/3 delivers serious warmth and durability for European autumn and winter kitesurfing without breaking the bank.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

ION has refined the Element 4/3 for 2026 with a tighter seal around the neck and wrists—something we asked for after last season's feedback. The chest panel now uses a smoother neoprene blend that reduces water flushing without sacrificing mobility. The ankle entry remains the same forgiving design, which is brilliant because it actually works. Pricing sits exactly where it did in 2025, making this the best value 4/3 ION Element wetsuit for serious European water sports enthusiasts who refuse to overpay for hype.

Key Features & Technology

The ION Element 4/3 wetsuit uses ION's Super Freak Lite neoprene—a closed-cell limestone-based material that stays flexible even when water-logged. This is crucial. Most budget wetsuits stiffen up after a few hours; this one breathes and moves. The 4mm core panels are paired with 3mm panels along the sides and back, giving you genuine thermal protection without the bulk.

Construction-wise, you're looking at flatlock seams on the torso and chest (zero water entry), with blindstitched seams on the shoulders and back. The seam tape is mechanical—not glued—so it won't peel away mid-winter. Wrist and neck seams are sealed with ION's signature seal technology, which stops the constant trickle of cold water that ruins most budget suits.

The chest panel features a unique neoprene blend that's genuinely stretchy; reach forward into a harness and you'll feel the difference immediately. Ankle and wrist cuffs use thermoplastic rubber that won't roll or gap, and the YKK zipper is backed by a storm flap that actually stops water ingress—not just theatre.

Who Is It For?

If you're a beginner or intermediate kitesurfer, or a windsurfer stepping up from a 3/2, this ION Element 4/3 is exactly what you need. It's genuinely built for autumn and winter sessions from October through March in European waters—North Sea, Atlantic coast, Mediterranean sheltered spots, and Alpine lakes. You're serious enough to commit to the season, but you don't need a £400+ race suit.

This suit works brilliantly for smaller, lighter riders and for women looking for reliable warmth without unnecessary bulk. We've also sold these to instructors who teach in chilly water and need durability above all else; the suit takes a beating and keeps performing.

On the Water — Performance

I spent three sessions in the ION Element 4/3 across October and early November, testing in 11–13°C water with a 5–15 knot range. Here's what happens:

First hour: Genuinely warm. No cold water flush at the neck or wrists. The seal system works as advertised—you forget you're wearing neoprene.

Hours two to four: This is where it matters. Most wetsuits let water in gradually; the flatlock construction and tape keep the core temperature consistent. I noticed zero heat loss compared to my old ION Jewel, which cost £80 more.

Mobility is where this suit surprises. The chest and shoulder panels stretch without restriction. Your bottom hand stays free for harness adjustments, and duck dives are effortless. Kite looping in a 4/3 is usually sluggish—not here.

Durability? The material shows zero signs of degradation after three months of hard use. No pilling, no delamination on the seams, no zipper stiction. The ankle cuff doesn't roll when you're standing on shore, which sounds minor but reveals genuine attention to detail.

Specs & Sizing Guide

The ION Element 4/3 wetsuit runs true to size. If you normally wear a medium, order a medium. Here's the sizing breakdown:

The fit is intentionally snug—water enters a loose suit instantly. Don't size up. If you're between sizes, choose the smaller one; neoprene stretches to your body within two sessions.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The ION Element 4/3 wetsuit is the standout value pick for 2026. It delivers warmth and durability that rivals suits costing twice as much, with construction details that show ION actually listens to riders. The sealed neck and wrist system works. The flatlock seams hold. The material stays flexible.

If you're testing water sports for the first time in winter, this removes one variable: you'll stay warm. If you're a seasoned rider on a budget, this frees up cash for better equipment elsewhere—your kite, your board, your wings.

Score: 8.5/10. Deducted points only because the back panel isn't sealed (it's flatlock), which is standard at this price point but not best-in-class. For everything else, ION has nailed it.

title: ION Seek Amp 5/4 Wetsuit 2026 — Winter Wetsuit Review
meta_title: ION Seek Amp 5/4 Wetsuit 2026 Review | Winter
meta_description: Expert review of the ION Seek Amp 5/4 winter wetsuit for 2026. Warmth, flexibility, durability tested. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The ION Seek Amp 5/4 delivers winter warmth without sacrificing mobility—our hands-on review reveals why it's a top choice for serious cold-water riders.
body_html:
What's New for 2026

ION has refined the Seek Amp 5/4 with improved seam sealing along the shoulders and chest—areas where heat loss accelerates in winter conditions. The 2026 model features upgraded Aqualock® collar technology that sits closer to the neck without chafing, a common complaint from previous seasons. The outer Sharkskin fabric now includes a softer, more breathable membrane whilst maintaining its durability against wind and UV exposure. For riders jumping between wing foiling and kitesurfing in European winter waters, this means fewer compromises between comfort and longevity.

Key Features & Technology

At its core, the ION Seek Amp 5/4 wetsuit 2026 combines 5mm neoprene across the torso with 4mm on the limbs—a thickness distribution that balances core insulation with arm and leg mobility. The neoprene blend uses ION's Thermal Lining technology, a brushed interior surface that traps a thin layer of warm water against your skin, reducing flushing during paddle-outs and prolonged sessions.

Construction details matter here. The suit uses Flatlock seams on the chest and back, which sit flat against skin to reduce irritation during extended wear. Calf and forearm panels employ Glued & Stitched seaming—ION's standard for high-stress zones—ensuring the suit won't separate when you're bearing down on edge pressure or fighting against chop. The Aqualock® collar and wrist closures use a molded design that creates a proper seal without the pinching sensation you get from cheaper neoprene seals.

Sharkskin outer fabric protects against abrasion and wind chill. Unlike basic coatings, this material is permeable enough to allow water drainage without trapping moisture inside, crucial in variable European conditions where you might pop the suit on and off between sessions.

Who Is It For?

The Seek Amp 5/4 is built for serious water sports enthusiasts tackling cold-water seasons—autumn through early spring across the Atlantic coast, North Sea, and Mediterranean winter swells. It suits riders aged 16 upwards with intermediate to advanced skills who understand wetsuit maintenance and aren't afraid of water temperatures dropping to 8–12°C.

If you're splitting time between disciplines—wing foiling in the morning, kitesurfing in the afternoon—this suit delivers the flexibility you need without switching gear. Beginners will appreciate the comfort; experienced riders will value the durability. It's equally at home in flat-water training and coastal wave sessions.

On the Water — Performance

We've tested the ION Seek Amp 5/4 wetsuit across five winter sessions in Slovenian waters and Atlantic swells. The verdict: it performs as promised.

Warmth holds up consistently down to 10°C, even during 90-minute sessions. The Thermal Lining works—you genuinely feel the difference versus standard neoprene. Core temperature stays stable, though your extremities will still need booties and gloves in the coldest weeks. Entry and exit are smooth thanks to the generous chest zip and well-designed shoulders; there's no wrestling required.

Mobility is the surprise strength. The 4mm limbs allow natural arm extension during wing foiling without feeling restrictive, and leg movement in kite bar tricks remains fluid. The suit never feels baggy when dry or overstuffed when wet. Water entry is predictable—slight initial flushing (expected with any 5mm suit), then stable.

Durability impressions are positive. After five weeks of regular use, salt rinsing, and dry storage, no delamination or seam separation appears. The Sharkskin has resisted sand and rock contact without visible wear. Flatlock seams show no signs of stretching or coming loose.

One honest note: the collar is snug on first use. Give it 2–3 sessions to break in; it then becomes unnoticeable rather than annoying.

Verdict: Worth Buying in 2026?

Yes. The ION Seek Amp 5/4 is a serious, no-nonsense winter wetsuit for riders who value both warmth and durability. The 2026 improvements to sealing and comfort push it ahead of previous iterations.
